channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: The rewards come later
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
If there’s one thing teachers must learn to accept as part of the job, it’s delayed gratification. And maybe that’s too big a word to use for the little rewards that we come by, days, months, and even years later. But when those moments come, they do bring a warm glow to the heart and make up for the hours of what seems at times to be the routine drudgery of our everyday lives.

Many years ago, fuelled by a sense of total irritation with a group of students who had failed to take a deadline seriously, I dashed off (what I thought was) a sharply written note telling them what awaited them in the “real world” where such tardiness would not be excused nor tolerated, and warning them about the consequences of not attending to detail in substance and presentation. My general exasperation with their casual attitude to class assignments led me to throw in some general advice about the uncompromising nature of workplace expectations. I hit “send” on the email and waited, expecting some contrite apologies to trickle in. Even 24 hours later, not one from the group of 23 had acknowledged the email, let alone responded to it with something like an apology. Youngsters these days, I thought to myself, and put the incident behind me.

Five years later, a note popped into my inbox, from a sender whose name seemed familiar, but whose address looked associated with a distant foreign university. I opened it, anticipating a request for a letter of recommendation or some such. But no, it was a response to that email from all those years ago. This former student had just got a teaching job at a university overseas, and for some strange reason he decided to revisit that email from me. Reading it, he said, was like going through an orientation session before he embarked on his first job, and he was finally able to understand and see the relevance of what I had wanted to convey.

Often in the pages of Teacher Plus (or now, as you school through the website) we find accounts from individuals – some of them teachers – of how they went in a certain direction based on what they had heard from a teacher in primary school. Or how a lesson that went over their heads in a math class suddenly made sense years later, when they were in a situation where the principle was applied. Our effectiveness as teachers is almost always measured by performance in examinations, but we know that marks are not the best indicator of understanding, and that often takes years to gauge – and usually, we are not around to see the penny drop. It’s rare that we even hear directly about whether and how we have impacted learning. But when we do, the warmth of satisfaction is palpable.
item.excerpt:encoded: If there’s one thing teachers must learn to accept as part of the job, it’s delayed gratification. And maybe that’s too big a word to use for the little rewards that we come by, days, months, and even years later.

item.title: Bridging the cyber security skills gap in India: the role of industry-led education
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Karmendra Kohli

In the ever-evolving world of cyber threats, the skills required to defend against them must evolve just as fast. Yet, many academic programmes still rely on outdated curricula, producing graduates who are not job-ready. As a professional who has long operated at the intersection of industry and education, I believe that closing the cyber security skills gap in India requires robust collaboration between the corporate sector and academic institutions.

At the heart of the issue lies one fundamental truth: education must be industry-aligned. The world of cybersecurity changes rapidly, so course content and outlines must be periodically reviewed and updated. Technologies, attack vectors, and threat landscapes evolve by the week—sometimes even by the day—and it is crucial that the knowledge imparted in our classrooms reflects this dynamism.

We design modular courses that allow us to swiftly integrate new topics, tools, and threat models as they emerge. “Our students at SecurEyes are encouraged to think critically, to ask why something works the way it does. That mindset of questioning is key to staying updated in cybersecurity.

Learning by doing: internships and hands-on labs
A theory-heavy curriculum is not enough. Cybersecurity is a field that demands hands-on experience. Emphasis on live internships, which are offered to students who complete cybersecurity courses, ensures they gain hands-on experience with real-world threats and enterprise environments, effectively bridging the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical application.

We also invest heavily in lab infrastructure, enabling students to experiment, simulate attacks, and build defence mechanisms in a controlled environment. This real-time exposure is invaluable and is something no textbook can substitute.

 From curated curriculum to lifelong learning
One of the most important traits we try to instil in our learners is the spirit of self-learning. A cybersecurity graduate must understand that their education does not end with the course. If someone graduates today, their knowledge might be partially outdated in just a few months. That is the nature of this field. It is not about simply finishing a course. It is about cultivating the habit of self-driven learning—an attitude that keeps professionals sharp, current, and relevant.

Effective models of industry-academia collaboration
There are many models that can successfully bridge the academia-industry gap: internships, apprenticeships, curriculum co-creation, boot camps, and even university-industry certification programs. The key is to keep students in touch with the realities of the industry.

Cybersecurity training should follow a model that combines academic depth with real-world industry exposure. Ideally, such programs must be built in collaboration with industry partners who can bring in current, field-tested expertise.

For example, our partnership with universities to launch M.Sc. and M.Tech. programs in Cybersecurity reflects this approach. As knowledge partners, we ensure that students do not just learn theoretical concepts—they are exposed to real-world case studies, attend guest lectures from seasoned cybersecurity professionals, and gain hands-on experience in state-of-the-art lab environments. This kind of immersive, industry-integrated learning is essential to prepare professionals who are ready to handle modern-day cyber challenges from day one.

Beyond the tech: soft skills, ethics, and mind management
While technical training is vital, it is not the only skill cybersecurity professionals need. We cannot forget the human side of cybersecurity. Ethical considerations, soft skills, and a proactive security mindset are just as important as technical prowess.

We focus on training students to develop a positive and disciplined mindset. Mind management techniques, stress control, and even modules on meditation or yoga have found their place in our courses. People need to know how to stay calm and composed, especially when under cyberattack. These are high-stress jobs. If we can build resilient minds along with technical experts, we have done our job right.

Government and corporate collaboration
To truly scale impact, government departments, cybersecurity firms, and universities must work together. There is a massive demand-supply gap in cybersecurity talent, and only a collaborative effort can bridge it.

Measuring success in cybersecurity education
Cybersecurity training should be structured to not only build strong technical foundations but also assess a learner’s ability to apply knowledge in real-world scenarios. It should include rigorous assessments, group discussions, and project presentations, much like what we do in our flagship CSCP program (Cyber Security Certification Program). These elements help evaluate not just technical competence but also communication, critical thinking, and teamwork skills. The ultimate measure of success is employability. If learners are able to crack interviews, secure jobs, and thrive in their roles, that is when we know the training has truly made an impact.

Cybersecurity for all
Cybersecurity is not just for those who want to make a career in it. It is for everyone, from curious students to working professionals seeking to understand how to protect their digital presence.

The future of cybersecurity education lies in adaptability, collaboration, and a holistic approach to learning. Whether you are a student, an educator, or a professional, it is time we all come together to secure our digital future—one skill at a time.

Karmendra Kohli is the CEO & Director, SecurEyes, a pure-play cybersecurity consulting, services, and products company that also provides cybersecurity training and education. He can be reached at karmendra.kohli@secureyes.net.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Karmendra Kohli
In the digital world, cyber threats and attacks are a reality. Knowing about cybercrime and how to handle them is the need of the hour not just for someone who is looking to make a career halting cybercrime but for every person who has some kind of a digital presence.

item.title: Stepwells: a holistic view
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Shalini Sabikhi

A stepwell also known as a vav is an ancient source of water with steps leading down to the water. Stepwells are found in many regions in India, especially in the arid states of Rajasthan and Gujarat. A stepwell is an excellent example of rainwater harvesting followed in India in the ancient times. It provided water for irrigation, cooking, washing, and drinking. The temperature in a stepwell is 50 less than that on the ground. So, it served as a resting place for people, providing them respite from the heat and fruits from the orchard around the stepwell. Religious rituals were performed here and it was also a place to socialize for women. With the passage of time, stepwells fell into neglect.

Now with growing awareness, many stepwells are being revived and also being used for music festivals and photo shoots.

Some of the most famous stepwells in India are:

  • Rani ki Vav in Patan, Gujarat (UNESCO World Heritage site)
  • Adalaj Vav in Gandhinagar, Gujarat
  • Chand Baori in Abhaneri, Rajasthan
  • Dholavira Stepwell in Dholavira, Gujarat
  • Toorji ka Jhalra in Jodhpur, Rajasthan
  • Bai Harir Vav in Ahmedabad, Gujarat

Stepwells can become an excellent source of learning for school students. There is something to learn in each subject from a stepwell. So, a theme exhibition on stepwells will indeed be enlightening and educational. This theme can be announced at the beginning of the academic session (for class7 or above) so that the students get enough time to read, visit, research, and prepare for the exhibition to be held at the end of the year. Field trips to stepwells can also be organized by the school.

For the exhibition, different rooms or spaces can be provided for different subjects.

In Room 1, students can do a role play about how people used stepwells in the past--women would come to fill water and spend time with each other, pilgrims, traders, and travellers would come to quench their thirst, rest, and eat, Some people would conduct religious rituals. Students can share about the culture and society of the past, the importance of water in daily life and how stepwells have helped sustain civilizations.

Room 2 can display charts on various stepwells with pictures and information.

Room 3 can be dedicated to mathematics, where students can share about symmetry in the steps and wells, mathematical formulas for the heights of steps, angles at which they are made, heights of the columns, proportions in the steps, geometric shapes and patterns, etc. 

Room 4 can be for history. Students can talk about ancient Indian engineering and architecture, which include the intricate designs, craftsmanship, and functional use of stepwells. They can also share about the historical periods during which stepwells were constructed and their role in trade, religion, and daily life.

Room 5 can be for environmental awareness, sustainability, and water management.  Understanding how stepwells were built in harmony with Nature will encourage students to consider sustainable practices in modern times. Stepwells were sustainable sources of water ensuring people had enough water throughout the year. They make use of innovative water conservation methods such as storing and managing water during droughts and dry seasons. Students can share about this very important aspect, which is a need of the present times when the groundwater level is receding in many cities. They can share ideas on how to make stepwells functional once again and other innovative methods for rainwater harvesting.

Room 6 for geography. The location of a stepwell depends on the climate, availability of construction materials, and presence of a natural source of water like a river, lake, or underground aquifer. Students can share their learnings in geography from stepwells.

Room 7 for architecture. The multistory designs, often with complex carvings and sculptures, can inspire students to make presentations on architectural innovation and creativity. Students can also share about the arches, columns, carvings, etc. They can make models of stepwells, including the different types named on the basis of the number of entrances.

Room 8 for sociopolitical influence. Stepwells were often commissioned by women or rulers reflecting the political and social conditions of the time. It was a place of interaction between different classes. Students can share why the people then thought that constructing stepwells and providing water was a pious act.

Room 9 for art. Many stepwells have intricate carvings and sculptures, from which students can learn about the artistic skills of the people of the past. They can display sketches of various stepwells. They can share fun facts like how the carvings in Rani ki Vav in Patan are similar to the Patola designs on sarees.

Room 10 for religious significance. Students can share how water has always been sacred for various civilizations. Religious rituals were performed in stepwells and the water was believed to have healing properties. Students can also share about the rituals and local beliefs.

Room 11 for preservation and conservation. Students can share about the importance of conserving historical monuments for future generations. They can also share about how they helped in cleaning up neglected structures as part of community service, try to get the required permissions to sow seeds that will grow into fruit bearing trees, so that the surrounding orchards can be revived and suggest methods by which stepwells can be recharged with water.

Room 12 for tourism and economy. Stepwells are a tourist attraction and they add to the economy. Thus, students can share about the role of tourism on local economies and how they help a number of people earn-- hotels, cafes, tour guides, souvenir shops, local crafts stores, photographers, tour operators, etc.

Room 13 to display photographs that the students clicked on their visit to various stepwells.

Room 14 for showcasing how music festivals are held in stepwells in the present times. Students can put up a classical/folk music and dance show against the backdrop of a stepwell.

Room 15 for books on stepwells. Tie up with a book seller and he can bring various books on stepwells to be browsed and bought.

Such an exhibition will provide a holistic view of what students can learn from stepwells, combining mathematical, historical, cultural, environmental, art, dramatics, music, and architectural lessons.

Book suggestions 

  • The Vanishing Stepwells of India-Victoria Lautman
  • Steps to Water- Morna Livingston
  • To Reach the Source- Claudio Cambon
  • Her Space, Her Story-Purnima Mehta Bhatt
  • Underground Shrine-Jaikishandas Sadani
  • Lesser Known stepwells in and around Ahmedabad-Gandhinagar region-Urban Management Centre

Shalini Sabikhi is a social studies educator in Ahmedabad.  She is interested in reading, travelling, gardening, art and craft, learning and sharing her learnings with others. She can be reached at svs502@yahoo.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shalini Sabhiki
That arch from a bygone era, under which you pass by every day to school, those remnants of a Mughal chieftain's palace , or the tombs of kings and queens, these are not just relics of the past, these monuments can teach us quite a lot and not just history. Shalini Sabikhi shows how stepwells can be a wealth of knowledge.

item.title: Let’s have fun with circle time!
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sunita Ahuja

Circle time is a foundational part of early childhood education that nurtures social, emotional, and cognitive development. Through engaging activities like puppet shows, storytelling, rhymes, action songs, and imaginative play, children build essential skills in a joyful and interactive setting. This article explores how to make circle time meaningful and magical by weaving fun and learning together using the world around children as a source of wonder and creativity.


Puppet play
Puppets are an excellent tool for grabbing children's attention and inspiring storytelling. Teachers can create and introduce a “Circle Time Friend” like Milo the Monkey or Tina the Turtle, who greets the children each day and shares little stories or riddles. Puppets can also act out social scenarios, teach values, and encourage children to participate by speaking or responding.
Tip: Use hand puppets, stick puppets, or even sock puppets. Let children take turns being the “puppeteer.”

Stories that spark imagination
Storytelling fuels children’s imagination and vocabulary. During circle time, teachers can read from books, narrate picture-free stories, or even create collaborative tales with inputs from the children.
Activity: Start a story with “Once upon a time, there was a magical tree…” and let the children suggest what happens next. This builds creativity and confidence.

Rhymes and rhythm
Rhymes and chants develop phonemic awareness and memory. Classic rhymes like “Twinkle Twinkle” or action rhymes like “Head, Shoulders, Knees and Toes” are perfect for circle time. They also support coordination and language development.
Extension: Add musical instruments like tambourines or maracas for extra fun!

Movement and expression
Children learn best when their whole bodies are engaged. Action songs like “If You’re Happy and You Know It” or “The Hokey Pokey” combine rhythm with gross motor movement, making learning a full-body experience.
Bonus: Create a “movement dice” with actions like jump, wiggle, dance, spin, freeze!

Exploring the world around us
Nature themes, animal movements, weather songs, or daily observations about the environment help children connect with the world. Discuss the season, day of the week, or talk about animals, insects, and flowers.
Activity: Bring a “mystery object” from Nature (like a pinecone or leaf) and let children guess what it is or share what they know about it.

Let imagination soar
Use pretend play and imaginative scenarios. “Let’s fly to the moon today” or “Let’s visit the jungle” are fun ways to transport kids to new adventures. Props like scarves, hats, or masks enhance the experience.

Circle time activities can be brain boosting for small kids and help in cognitive development and critical thinking..Circle time is not only about sitting in a circle but it is about listening to children’s experiences and sharing a lot of amazing things with them.

Sunita Ahuja has been a passionate teacher for about 17 years. Currently, she is working as a pre-school teacher in Reliance Foundation School, Mouda. She can be reached at sunitaahuja700@gmail.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sunita Ahuja
Circle time is a good way of incorporating some fun into learning. Here are a few activities that you can try with your little ones.

item.title: School politics: the elephant in the room
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Akanksha

They say, “A good teacher is like a candle who consumes itself to light the way for others.“ But what happens when the candle itself struggles to light up and has to depend on others for its existence? Teaching is considered a noble profession, but does this mean that employers can exploit teachers for their own benefits? Teachers, today, are trying to stay afloat in an environment that is anything but noble.

Once upon a time, a passionate and enthusiastic teacher joined her first school with high hopes for a bright career, only to learn that she is jumping into a vicious cycle of jealousy, competition, and insecurity. She got an opportunity to teach the subject she was passionate about and competent in. But her enthusiasm soon faded away. She learnt that her colleagues were not comfortable with her. She was questioned at every step, from creating lesson plans to making notes, so much so that she started doubting her own ability to teach. Frustration began to set in. She tried to reach a higher authority for support, only to find that this is a game of people-pleasing and survival. Her subject was looked down upon and often criticized.

It is not just the students, but teachers are also subjected to the science > commerce > humanities hierarchy. Parents and schools have already labelled some subjects as CORE and others as peripheral. Your value is also determined accordingly. Even within the subject groups there is hierarchy. English teachers look down on the other subjects in the humanities group as being easy to teach and assess, but it is always the English teachers who are in the limelight—they get opportunities to speak at forums on behalf of the school or be the hosts for events in the school. The new teacher was having a tough time. She was being questioned, the papers she set were being rejected, and her classroom management skills were never “up to the mark” and if she dared point out any mistakes made by the senior teachers, she saw their wrath.

She was slowly adjusting to this toxicity, as after all, it is the survival of the fittest, right? Soon, she started realizing that her hard work and dedication was not being recognized as she expected. There was no concept of appraisal as per one’s performance. She was intentionally given lower grades to teach inspite of graduating from one of the most renowned institutions in the country and having all the necessary skills to teach the higher grades. Finally, she decided to leave the school. She joined a “reputed” institution in the city. The first few days were exciting, but soon the vicious cycle of politics started rising its head again. This time it was subtle and refined with multiple layers which can put even corporate politics to shame.

Her patience was being tested every day. She started doubting her career choice. She was not enthusiastic about her job anymore, even though she still loved her subject and students. School politics was draining her; she was unable to develop a thick skin. People-pleasing was not her cup of tea. In the end, she was defeated -- defeated by seniors who could not see her getting the limelight , defeated by colleagues who could only think about their benefit, and defeated by “friends” who were ready to backstab just to please the authority. She resigned with a very heavy heart, paying three months of her salary as per the rule and was blamed by the institution for her impulsive decision. Was this decision impulsive? I leave you to think….

This is not the story of one teacher only. Education has become a business. India used to be a land of gurus who were given the highest respect, but today this profession is reduced to delivering lessons, paper checking, management, and parent-pleasing. It has no soul. It feels no less than a slaughter house of people who choose this profession for the betterment of society and job satisfaction, but end up getting butchered at the hands of people who think they know the real meaning of education.

Akanksha is a freelance educator based in Bengaluru. She graduated in geography (hons.) from the prestigious Miranda House, Delhi University. She has 8 years of teaching experience. Currently, she is working as a geography teacher and UPSC coach in Bengaluru. She is passionate about her subject and is working to develop a good pedagogy and resources for teaching-learning geography. She can be reached at akankshaakki1416@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Akanksha
Teaching they say is a noble profession. But in a sacred environment, when politics makes an entry, dignity, respect, and professionalism make their exit, leaving the noble profession murky.

item.title: Bringing stories to life: engaging young minds through storytelling
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Usha Rani Ravela
As a primary educator, I have always been fascinated by the power of storytelling in shaping young minds. Storytelling is not just about narrating events; it is an immersive experience that allows children to connect, imagine, and retain concepts for the long run. This became evident to me when I introduced storytelling through role play, sequencing, and story mapping in my class. The results were profound – not only did the children engage deeply with the stories, they also demonstrated a remarkable ability to recall and interpret them in their own unique ways.

Activity overview

To make storytelling more interactive and meaningful, I used a combination of three strategies:

  1. Role play: Children acted out different characters, helping them embody the emotions and actions of the story.
  2. Sequencing: We used picture cards to arrange story events in order, reinforcing the logical flow.
  3. Story mapping: Children created visual representations of the story, linking characters, settings, and key events.

For one of our sessions, I narrated a simple story about a clever mouse escaping danger. As I told the story, the children eagerly took on roles – some pretended to be the tiny, nervous mouse, others hissed like the rattlesnake, and a few played the fast-running coyote. They laughed, expressed different emotions, and even added their own ideas to make the story more exciting.

After the role play, we moved to sequencing, where the children arranged illustrated flashcards to match the story’s events. This step helped them understand how a story flows from beginning to end. Finally, through story mapping, they drew their own versions of the adventure, adding personal touches that reflected their unique interpretations.

Insights and observations

What struck me most was how engaged the children were. The mix of movement, visuals, and storytelling made the experience unforgettable. Days later, when I asked them to retell the story, they remembered all the details and even added creative twists. One child imagined the mouse tricking many animals, while another described the snake learning a lesson and becoming kinder.

This experience reinforced an important lesson: storytelling is not about passive listening but about active exploration. Just like children's artworks reflect their thoughts and emotions, their engagement with stories reveals their unique perspectives. Their versions may not always match the original, but they carry deep personal meaning.

Why it’s important to use interactive storytelling

Engaging children through storytelling techniques aligns with research on early learning. According to Wells (1986), “Children construct meaning through interaction, not just observation.” This means that the more they engage with a story through different senses – movement, visuals, and discussion – the better they understand and retain it.

Loris Malaguzzi, founder of the Reggio Emilia approach, beautifully stated: “Children have a hundred languages, and a hundred more to think, to play, to speak…”

Storytelling allows children to use these ‘languages’ – acting, drawing, speaking, and imagining – to express their understanding. Encouraging these diverse expressions fosters confidence, creativity, and deeper connections with the learning material.

The Importance of open interpretation

Much like abstract drawings in children’s art, storytelling does not have to be rigidly structured. What may seem like a simple story to an adult can hold layers of meaning for a child. By allowing them to explore, re-imagine, and retell stories in their own ways, we honour their perspectives and thought processes.

As educators, we must approach storytelling with curiosity rather than correction, encouraging children to share their interpretations without the fear of being ‘wrong.’ Open-ended questions such as “What do you think happened next?” or “Why do you think the bird felt that way?” allow children to dive deeper into their imaginations and express their ideas freely.

Conclusion

Storytelling, when enriched with interactive strategies, becomes more than just an academic exercise – it becomes a gateway into a child’s thoughts, emotions, and creativity. The difference in how children and adults perceive a story isn’t a gap to be bridged, but an opportunity to celebrate diverse perspectives.

By embracing role play, sequencing, and story mapping, we create a learning environment where children feel empowered to express, imagine, and remember. Their stories may take unexpected turns, but in those twists and deviations, we find the magic of their boundless creativity.

Usha Rani Ravela is an enthusiastic educator with 20 years of teaching experience. An Innovative Ambassador for Design Thinking and Innovation by the Ministry of Education, she has conducted webinars on pedagogy and assessment. Through creative methods, she helps children gain confidence and believe in their abilities. She can be reached at usharaniravela20@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Usha Rani Ravela
Children have “a hundred languages” to express themselves, and storytelling taps them all. Usha Rani shows how children explore, interpret, and express their ideas, turning simple tales into unforgettable learning experiences.

item.title: From fire to ice: how competitions shape us and how they can heal us
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Kalpana Sharma

For some students, competitions unlock confidence and skills textbooks rarely teach. This is what I believed once upon a time as a student and then as a teacher in the formative years of my teaching. I would be very prompt and active and grab every opportunity to prepare my students to stand out in various competitions. Looking back now, after a decade at a Krishnamurti school, I sometimes laugh at myself. What marathon was I running? It felt like an obsession – chasing crowns one after another, never satisfied. And yet, many of my students remain grateful for that ride.

“I was terrified of public speaking until I participated in the State level debate competition. Now I can argue my point in front of 200 people without shaking,” recalls Vasu Bhardwaj, a class 10 student, 2005, BSN Shimla.

Others describe competitions as their first encounter with global issues.

“Representing BSN at the National Children’s Science Congress in Sikkim made me read about environmental agreements for the first time. It felt like real-world learning,” says Preetinder Singh, class 10, 2000.

Here, the enrichment is clear: critical thinking, teamwork, and curiosity flourish.

The prestige pressure

But for many, the story is different. Schools often handpick a small group of “competition stars” and pour resources into their success. This is exactly what I was doing.

“It felt like we weren’t representing ourselves – we were representing the school’s reputation. Winning mattered more than learning,” says Saurav Verma, class 9, 2004, BSN Shimla.

Students also feel excluded.

“Unless you were already a topper or had polished English, you never got chosen. Most of us never even knew when the Olympiad sign-ups happened,” shares a student from my previous school.

This raises the question: are competitions really for the students or for the school brochure? This is exactly what urges me to question the approach I was living with in my formative teaching years.

Inequality at play

Access is another issue. Private schools can hire debate coaches and send students to international MUNs. Government schools often can’t. This realization came when I was interacting with my caretaker’s son studying in a senior class in a government school.

“When we finally went for a quiz contest, the other team had custom jackets and coaches. We had no practice and nothing to rely on,” says Lovely, class 9, from a government school.

Such disparities make “merit” look more like privilege.

The wellbeing question

Even for those who get in, the pressure can be crushing.

“I pulled three months before the State Level Quiz. I didn’t care about anything else. My only focus was winning the crown,” says Kulvinder, class 10.

Some thrive under this intensity. But others burn out or feel invisible when their school values only winners.

A way forward

Competitions don’t have to be either enriching or performative. They can be both – if schools change how they approach them. Opening participation beyond the “star students,” valuing effort over trophies, and connecting events back to classroom learning can ensure all students benefit. Once, a student of mine told me, “I don’t mind if the school posts our photos on social media or we get the news coverage, but I wish they cared as much about the kids who didn’t win as the ones who did.”

An eye-opener

My perspective shifted most profoundly during a cultural exchange at Rajghat Besant School, Varanasi. Selected by SEEDS Asia, two schools from India interacted with two from Japan on the theme of sustainability.

What struck me was the ease with which the Japanese students participated. They questioned and answered spontaneously, always in groups, listening to one another and supporting each other. There was no identity crisis, no one-upmanship. The thrust was on learning together.

I was so moved by the approach that I started to read about various educational models across the globe. Sharing a few here:

Japan: Group effort and belonging

Japan’s educational approach revolves around group effort and belonging. School life is built around bukatsu (after-school clubs), where collective perseverance (ganbaru) matters more than victory. As educator Tsunesaburo Makiguchi noted: “Education is not to prepare for success, but to create value in life.” Yet, Japan also faces the shadow of juken jigoku (“examination hell”), where relentless test preparation overshadows joy.

Finland: Equity over competition

Finland, often ranked among the world’s best systems, avoids high-stakes testing and inter-school competitions. Equity, trust, and student wellbeing take precedence. After reading the book, Teach like Finland, I gathered that the emphasis there is not to prepare children to be the best in the world, but to prepare them to be the best for the world. Here, enrichment is measured not by trophies but by how inclusive and stress-free learning remains.

Reggio Emilia and Summerhill: Children as agents

The Reggio Emilia approach in Italy treats children as co-constructors of knowledge. Projects emerge from curiosity and collaboration; learning is visible but not ranked.

In England, A.S. Neill’s Summerhill School pushes this further with democratic, non-compulsory classes. Competitions are irrelevant because choice and joy, not achievement, are the goal. Neill insisted: “The function of the child is to live his own life – not the life that his anxious parents think he should live.”

Fire and ice: education’s balance

All this has made me pause. And I have translated this into my question: Why have I been educated? What am I doing as a teacher?

I happen to be from the hills, and we are all aware of the recent disasters in the mountain areas of our country. This summer when I visited my hometown, a sad thought overpowered me after seeing things withering away all around. Cement construction everywhere, deep scars left by the fury of nature. In spite of all this, everyone seemed comfortable with this “new normal,” continuing in the race of winning.

The first thing I did was visit my alma mater. I asked the principal to give me a slot to interact with the senior grade students, where I wished to explore this question with them. Together, we began reflecting on the difference between studying only for marks, medals, and glories versus learning for something deeper.

During the session, we dipped further to evolve our Ikigai. Ikigai, a Japanese concept, simply means “a reason for being.” It lies at the intersection of four guiding questions:

  • What do I love to do?
  • What am I good at?
  • What does the world need?
  • What can I be paid for?

When children and teachers started mapping their thoughts on these questions, it became a powerful moment. They could begin to understand and even question the true purpose of being educated.

Further into the sessions, I began a discussion on Robert Frost’s Fire and Ice. The poem is simple on the surface – fire as desire, ice as hatred – but when I asked, “What are we really being educated for?” the group fell silent.

Are we being trained only to win debates, hackathons, MUNs, and Olympiads – to showcase brilliance and bring prestige to our schools? Or are we being educated to confront the realities that shape our future: climate change, wars, displacement, and disasters?

I had to help students and teachers understand that Frost’s metaphors are no longer just poetic. Rising global temperatures are quite literally “fire.” Melting polar ice caps are not just “ice” but the collapse of ecosystems. Hatred, intolerance, and violence fuel wars that students read about daily in the news.

In that moment, something powerful flowed. The group began to see that education cannot be reduced to medals or certificates. The reflection that emerged was this: The real purpose of learning is not performance for prestige but preparation for responsibility – to think critically, to care deeply, and to act wisely in a fractured world.

Competitions might sharpen skills, but if they do not help students ask the right questions – What do my choices mean for the planet? How does my voice counter hatred? How can I act with empathy when disasters strike? – Then education risks becoming ornamental rather than transformative.

So the challenge remains: Do our school events and classrooms prepare us for the headlines we will inherit, or are we being educated only to decorate report cards?

As Frost suggests, the world may end in fire or in ice. But perhaps the more urgent question for students and teachers alike is: Will our education give us the courage to keep it alive?

Fire and ice, competitions, and the balance of education

In our schools, we often see these forces at play. Fire is the rush to top exams, to win Olympiads, debates, MUN awards, or hackathons. Ice is the silent exclusion of those who cannot compete – the cold comparisons, the apathy towards anyone left behind.

But must education be only about feeding fire?

Competition versus excellence

A key distinction is often overlooked: competition is about beating others; excellence is about surpassing yourself.

  • Competition can ignite fire, but it can also lead to stress, comparison, and ego-driven pursuits.
  • Excellence, on the other hand, channels ambition into growth, curiosity, and skill-building – even if there is no trophy.
  • Schools can guide students to focus on excellence, turning competitions into exercises in self-improvement, collaboration, and meaningful contribution, rather than mere prestige.

When guided well, these events train students not for prestige, but for participation in healing the world. The mathematics they learn may one day model climate solutions. The negotiation skills honed in MUNs may help prevent wars. The empathy practised in debates may cool the ice of hatred. The curiosity nurtured in Olympiads may ignite discoveries that serve humanity, not greed.

So, must competitions end in fire or ice? Not necessarily. The same events that sometimes fuel ego and prestige can also become platforms of healing – if reimagined with balance.

The question, “What are we being educated for?” does not ask us to abandon competitions altogether. It asks us to rebalance them – to ensure fire does not become greed, ice does not become hatred, and that every skill, every lesson, and every victory becomes a step towards making this world more just, more peaceful, and more alive.

Frost reminds us that the world could end in fire or in ice. Education, if held in balance, gives us another possibility: a world sustained by knowledge, warmed by compassion, and made better by the hands and hearts of those who learned not just to win, but to heal.

“Not the trophy, not the race,
But the kindness in the pace.
Win or lose, the measure true,
Is how we cared in all we do.” – A student

Tagore and Gandhi: harmony over rivalry

Rabindranath Tagore rejected rigid competition, seeing education as a process of self-expression in harmony with nature. “The highest education is that which does not merely give us information but makes our life in harmony with all existence.”

Similarly, Gandhi’s Nai Talim emphasized learning through craft, self-reliance, and community service. He warned against ranking that divided children. “By education, I mean an all-round drawing out of the best in the child and man – body, mind, and spirit.”

From their perspective, Olympiads and MUNs risk privileging intellect and external validation over creativity, service, and self-realization.

Steiner and Montessori: Freedom and creativity

Rudolf Steiner’s Waldorf philosophy prioritizes imagination, art, and rhythm in learning. Competition, he argued, undermines individuality.

Maria Montessori also placed the child’s inner development at the center: “Free the child’s potential, and you will transform him into the world.” Montessori classrooms emphasize mastery at one’s own pace, not winning over others.

Competitions here might be reframed not as tests of rank but as collaborative exhibitions of skill.

Krishnamurti: The critique of comparison

J. Krishnamurti was radical in his rejection of competition. “The moment you compare yourself with another, you destroy yourself.” Many Krishnamurti schools in India avoid competitions entirely.

A teacher in a Krishnamurti School explains:

“The moment you make learning about winning or losing, you introduce fear and comparison. We try to create a space where a child explores out of curiosity, not because a medal is at stake.” Ritu Srivasta, senior teacher at Rajghat Besant School.

Students here describe freedom rather than exclusion.

“At first I felt left out when my friends in other schools posted photos from MUNs. But then I realized I was exploring science fairs and theatre without worrying about judges. It feels freer,” says a class 9 student at Rajghat.

Rethinking competitions

Competitions are not inherently good or bad – they mirror the educational philosophy behind them. In some contexts, they cultivate confidence, exposure, and teamwork. In others, they reproduce inequality, stress, and prestige culture.

If schools want competitions to be genuinely enriching, they must shift the emphasis from winning to learning, from prestige to participation, from individual rivalry to collective growth.

Perhaps the last word belongs to Tagore, who insisted that true education aligns our lives “in harmony with all existence.” Competitions, when seen as shared journeys of discovery rather than trophies of pride, can move closer to that vision.

Example from Krishnamurti Schools / Rajghat Besant:

  • Instead of a debate “tournament,” students hold dialogue circles on global issues. They research, present, and listen, then collaboratively draft a “community resolution” like in MUN.
  • For science or coding, instead of a hackathon prize, students work together on projects to solve real environmental problems and then share the results with the local community or their parents.
  • In a Model United Nations, students don’t just perform speeches; they learn the art of negotiation, of listening to perspectives beyond their own, of empathizing with nations in crisis.
  • In a debate, beyond winning points, students can practice respectful disagreement and the courage to stand for truth.
  • In an Olympiad, what matters is not just solving problems faster than others, but learning perseverance, collaboration, and joy in discovery.
  • In a hackathon, teamwork, creativity, and innovation matter far more than any prize – because the real world will need these to solve climate emergencies, refugee crises, and disasters.

Rethinking competitions: a teacher’s guide

If you’re organizing a debate / hackathon / Olympiad – try this instead:

Focus on learning, not winning

  • Celebrate effort, improvement, and creativity, not only the final scores.
  • Add awards like: “Best Collaboration,” “Most Empathetic Argument,” or “Most Innovative Idea.”
  • Include short reflection circles: students share what they learned about teamwork, problem-solving, or themselves.

Make events collaborative and inclusive

  • Rotate roles so every student tries speaking, moderating, or note-taking.
  • Encourage team-based projects that address real-world problems.
  • Host school-wide festivals, where all forms of expression – science, tech, art, and storytelling – get equal space.

Connect recognition to purpose

  • Reward community impact and meaningful contributions, not just give medals.
  • Encourage mentorship: past participants guide newcomers.
  • Involve students in co-designing rules and judging criteria, giving them ownership and voice.

Shift the spotlight from podiums to processes – keep the excitement alive, but root it in inclusion, growth, and community impact.

Educating for the future: NEP, AI, and ethics

As we look ahead, the National Education Policy (NEP 2020) reminds us that education must be holistic, multidisciplinary, and rooted in social, emotional, and ethical learning. In a world increasingly shaped by Artificial Intelligence, automation, and rapid technological change, knowledge alone is not enough. Students must also develop empathy, ethical reasoning, collaboration, and creativity – skills that no AI can replace.

Competitions, projects, and classroom experiences should not merely reward speed or accuracy, but encourage curiosity, moral courage, and imagination. A student coding an AI model for climate solutions should also reflect on its social impact. A debate on global policy should teach negotiation and empathy. In this vision, education becomes a laboratory for developing human values alongside technical skills, preparing students not just to thrive in the future, but to shape it responsibly.

As Dr. Jane Goodall reminds us:
"What you do makes a difference, and you have to decide what kind of difference you want to make."

Let our schools, our classrooms, and our competitions nurture not just winners, but thoughtful, compassionate, and creative citizens – the generation that will heal the world rather than compete to conquer it.

Kalpana Sharma is an educator with over 25 years of experience in teaching and school leadership. Born in Shimla, Himachal Pradesh, she has worked with a variety of schools, colleges, and educational organizations. She served as Principal at BSN Shimla before joining Rajghat Besant School, a Krishnamurti institution, a decade ago. Over the years, she has held multiple roles including Coordinator, Headmistress, and is now Head of Social-Emotional Wellbeing and Inclusive Education at Rajghat Besant School. Kalpana has a deep passion for inclusive education and has actively worked with schools across India to foster this spirit. She leads Project Naman, (https://keekli.in/dav-totu-organised-a-workshop-on-inclusive-education/ and https://keekli.in/education-beyond-books-loreto-students-explore-the-true-purpose-of-learning/) through which she has conducted free workshops on inclusive education, social-emotional learning, and ethical wellbeing for students, parents, and teachers in 15 schools in Himachal. She continues to teach English and work closely with students with special needs, inspiring both learners and educators with her commitment to holistic, value-driven education. She can be reached at kalpana.sharma@rajghatbesantschool.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kalpana Sharma
Are school competitions shaping children’s skills or adding to their stress? Kalpana Sharma reflects on her teaching years, student stories, and global models to explore how contests can teach important skills, beyond merely winning.

item.title: Social contract in education
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

As we move towards 2030, we must prepare ourselves to answer new questions. What will be the nature and state of the world our students will live in? Are we readying them for the jobs and skills they will need not just to survive but to thrive in that new world? Do we understand the degree of the knowledge gap that a forward-looking education system will be expected to bridge? How can students and educators work towards such a future?

When the pandemic upstaged us by proving our fragility, it also framed our essential connectedness. The looming threat forced us to reimagine our future with collaboration at its centre. And the push came from UNESCO’s report titled ‘Reimaging our futures together: a new social contract for Education’. The report proposed that education could be viewed as a social contract in which there is an implicit agreement among members of a community, particularly educators and their students. It was argued that given the power of education to bring about profound change, a new social contract for education could repair past injustices while transforming the future.

This new social contract presented education as a public endeavour and a common good. However, the visions, principles, and proposals were put forth only as a starting point of a vital conversation. Born of a global consultation involving more than one million people, the new social contract for education was envisaged to help build a peaceful, just, and sustainable future for all.

COVID-19 had fundamentally and undoubtedly changed our educational experiences and notions of leadership. The new social contract in education tweaked the relationship between teachers, students, and knowledge, for instance. And one of the most succinct definitions of this new educator-student role came from Professor Yong Zhao of the School of Education at the University of Kansas and Dr Jim Watterston of the Melbourne Graduate School of Education in Australia. They said:

“Teachers no longer need to serve as the instructor, the sole commander of information to teach the students content and skills. Instead, the teacher serves other more important roles such as organizer of learning, curator of learning resources, counselor to students, community organizer, motivator and project managers of students’ learning.”

Zhao and Watterston also argued:

“It is incumbent upon all educators to use this crisis-driven opportunity to push for significant shifts in almost every aspect of Education: what, how, where, who, and when. In other words, Education, from curriculum to pedagogy, from teacher to learner, from learning to assessment, and from location to time, can and should radically transform.”

UNESCO’s report clearly and firmly placed educators at the centre of education, but what about the roles and responsibilities of the student in the social contract? Would ‘student-centered learning’ continue to be a passive concept, or would a student’s functional role be redefined? How could an educator lead an educational change that encouraged and supported students in playing a proactive role in learning? Did we even need the traditional classroom?

Adventurous, captivating, and flexible. These three words describe what education is expected to be in the future. A paper co-authored by Zhao and Watterston identifies three major changes that should happen in education in the era of machine learning and AI integration.

The first emphasizes that content should highlight creativity, critical thinking, and entrepreneurship, rather than gathering and hoarding information. Humans cannot thrive by competing with machines; they need to be more human instead.

The second advocates greater control over their learning for the students. The teacher’s role will then shift from instructor to curator of learning resources, counsellor, and motivator. This is the spot where “active learning” will kick in, with a growing body of research suggesting that comprehension and memory are better when students learn in a hands-on way – through discussion and interactive technologies.

The third proposal insists that learning should change – “from the classroom to the world”. With digital tools, it is no longer necessary for students to learn at the same time as each other. Imagine blended learning or a mix of online and face-to-face learning, also called the flipped classroom, where students read or watch lectures in their own time and solve problems in the presence of their teacher and peers.

The time has come perhaps for the decoupling of learning time and school time. And those involved are adjusting as students speed up their lectures, or lecturers begin dividing their presentations into five and ten-minute video segments. This could be the start of the end of the 45-minute period or the 50-minute lecture.

But what about those who don’t have the luxury of digital tools yet? The digital divide is not a new problem nor has it put a brake on change, “because the digital world moves faster in providing access than the physical one”.

As a matter of fact, the only way United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goal 4, which seeks to provide quality education for all by 2030, will be realized is if teachers in disadvantaged areas receive tools and materials digitally – perhaps via massive open online courses – and then pass them on to their students in the traditional way.

So, even if the digital divide won’t hold back the coming revolution, it seems unlikely that the classroom will ever look the same again.

Neerja Singh is a former teacher/journalist, published author and professional speaker on generational diversity with a background and training in media, having worked in advertising, public relations, documentary film making, and feature journalism. She is a TEDx speaker and represents the Professional Speakers Association of India on the Global Speakers Federation Board. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
What will a classroom look like in 2030? Neerja Singh examines UNESCO’s social contract for education, student-centered learning, and the evolving role of teachers in a digital, AI-driven world.

item.title: Could our education system be transformed by doing less?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Tulika Verma

As I write this, I am sitting across from a footwear shop. A toddler who seems about two years old and his mother are leaving the shop and coming down the stairs. The toddler slips and lands on his buttocks but gets up right away. His mother, understandably, tries to hold his hand but he pulls away decidedly, and continues to walk down the rest of the stairs on his own, slowly and unsteadily.

I see this and think about the innate agency in children and the dignity they associate with it. They protect it fiercely, asking through their actions, for the space to make their own mistakes and learn from them. I feel the same fierce sense of wanting to assert my agency as a 36-year old woman, and in my 84-year old grandmother, who, much like the toddler, sometimes stumbles a bit when she walks, but only extends a hand to ask for support when most needed.

If this desire for agency is so innate to human beings, why have we created an education system that strips children and adults alike of it? Why have we created classrooms that require children to be passive vessels where knowledge must be poured from the outside rather than acknowledge them as living, breathing beings who have an innate curiosity and capacity to learn? Why have we created education systems where teachers and others in the hierarchy all feel a sense of helplessness – that they must comply, must do what they are being asked to do?

The answers to many of these questions lie in the violent, colonial legacy of the education system as we know it. It is a system that defines the purpose of education as employability – an extremely low standard for a process as sacred and innate to the human experience as learning. It fails repeatedly in that low objective too, as ASER reports or national employability surveys will show you. It is based on a view of human beings as ‘resources’ for an industrial system that benefits a few. Therefore, it fails to see our humanity. On the one hand, it creates a sense of ‘each one for themselves’ as reinforced by the relentless focus on competition, minimizing the focus on developing our ability to collaborate and co-create. On the other, it suppresses, with an iron hand, individual creativity and independent thinking, by mandating singular ‘right’ answers and ways of doing things.

There have been many attempts to ‘correct’ this system. Usually, motivated by the desire to improve the employability outcome, and therefore, test scores and attendance, these attempts usually lead to doing more of the same. The thinking is – let’s train teachers more, let’s mandate more things. The assumption is often that teachers are either lazy or incompetent or both, and that children have no agency. As a result, teachers all over the country feel the burden of frequent trainings and tools they didn’t ask for, which they feel don’t work in their contexts, but feel pressured to engage in. A process like this is bound to lead to disengagement.

What then is the way forward? As I frame this question, I want to take into account not just the past and present of our country and the world, but also the future. We stand at a crossroads in history where the human tendency to keep consuming and doing more has led to a multi-dimensional crisis. The climate crisis, depletion of planetary resources, rapidly growing inequality, wars, the mental health and loneliness epidemic, are all signs of failing systems. According to the 2023-24 UNDP Human Development Report, about half of the world's population does not feel in control of their own life. Over 70% feel that they have no say in how larger political and social decisions are taken. What then is the way forward for education in this context? What world are our learners inheriting? What relationship are they building with the human and non-human world? What beliefs are they inheriting about their own ability to create a new reality for themselves and the world? What is the purpose of education in this world that so direly needs healing? Is it enough to continue to aim loosely and ineffectively for individual employability?

If our collective wellbeing could be the central pursuit of education, we would begin to see that children are not only innately capable of learning, but it is their way of life. Learning is not something that happens only in classrooms. Children are always observing, experimenting, pushing boundaries, making meaning and learning, both individually and with each other. Our role as educators then, perhaps, is not to make them learn, but to remove barriers along this natural path of learning.

I have been a lover and an observer of the natural world all my life, both the human and the more than human world. I feel that the non-human world has much to teach us about sustained growth and resilience. It offers powerful metaphors that can simplify complex human phenomena. Permaculture is a beautiful example of experimenting with organic growth. A permaculture farmer does not look at a piece of land and ask, “What do I want to grow here?” Instead, she asks, “What grows here?” This shift allows her to not treat the land like a lifeless thing that must be filled with pesticides and fertilizers. It allows her to be in connection with the innate nature of the land and know that the most natural thing for it to do is to grow things. So she plants species that are native to that land, and sees her role as one who fosters this innate capacity. She ensures that there is water and sunlight, and then she sees the magic that happens in the land on its own. When nurtured in a way that is most natural to it, the land begins to regenerate. New species of plants and insects arrive. A self-balancing ecosystem is created where pesticides are not needed. Whatever dies is returned to the land as compost, so no external fertilizer is needed. The farmer’s effort is greatly reduced. Her role is to sense the need and then to respond to that need by removing barriers in the natural process of growth.

What if children as well as adults are also living systems capable of this growth on their own? What if the role of teachers is to trust the child’s innate capacity for growth, notice what their natural tendencies are and just remove barriers that get in the way of that growth? What if the only capacity we need to build at all levels in the education system is one of trust in each other’s agency and capacity?

“This is all well and good. But I work in a system that expects outcomes. I can’t just let go of those,” is a sentiment I often hear in my work with people in the education system. In fact, when we go deeper, this feeling is articulated as, “If I had my way, I would do things differently but I have pressure from those above me to operate in a certain way.” This sentiment, ironically, is echoed by teachers, school leaders, education officers, all the way to the top. Parents feel a similar pressure from the society around them. The little shoulders of our children carry all this pressure. However, when we pause and try to understand this ‘pressure from above’, we realize that it is felt at all levels of the education system, by all the people who are the system. There is a sense of learned helplessness, a lack of agency that we have normalized in this system.

What can be done then? Maybe that is not the right question. Maybe the right question is, what must we stop doing? I will not offer prescriptions, because top-down prescriptions by people removed from the ground is at the very heart of the problems that plague this system. However, I will invoke a dream. What if we paused and took a deep collective breath? What if we came together in local communities of children, parents, teachers, non-teaching staff, school leaders and simply listened to the experiences of our children? What if we asked ourselves what we could stop doing or do less of, and how we could ease the burden of our violent expectations? What if we committed to co-creating with the students every learning experience, every school structure, every policy that impacts them? What if classrooms became playgrounds of imagination for students, where the teacher senses the unique potential of each child and responds to it? What if we can collectively serve teachers to bring their most grounded, compassionate selves into classrooms so that they can listen better? What if every child begins to have an ecosystem around her that trusts her and her desire for learning and commits to clearing pathways to her growth?

Imagine how much more harmonious the education system can become if we saw it as a living, breathing system capable of reaching great potential if we stopped filling it with excess.

Tulika Verma is a writer, facilitator, and leadership coach whose work sits at the intersection of education and systems change. She is the founder of Sahaj Collective, where she supports leaders and communities to reimagine how we live, learn, and work together – shifting from extractive ways of organizing to more compassionate and regenerative ones. A former City Director at Teach For India, Tulika now designs learning journeys, coaches leaders, and writes on themes of agency, community, and the deep transformation of systems that shape our lives. To know more about her and the work she does visit tulikaverma.com and sahajcollective.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Tulika Verma
More rules, more tests, more pressure – does all this really help children learn? Tulika Verma reflects on how simplifying the curriculum and co-creating it with students could make education more humane, creative, as well as meaningful.

item.title: Reimagining classrooms through archives: a teacher’s reflection on the NCBS summer fellowship
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Vaishnavi Bhat

When I first walked into the Archives as part of the NCBS Archives Public Fellowship*, I felt both curious and overwhelmed. It was like stepping into a vast ocean of information – files, exhibits, letters, lesson plans, and notes from another time. Getting lost in the sheer volume of archival material was, in itself, an experience. There was so much to read, so much to learn, and so much to make sense of. As a teacher, I found myself constantly asking: What here can speak to children? What here can act as a pedagogical tool? What stories from this sea of information can help them better understand education in India, especially through the lens of policy, change, and lived experience?

At first, it was difficult to find something that felt relevant for classroom use. But this challenge also opened up new ways of thinking. I realized I wasn’t just being given documents, I was being given questions, and I was learning how to find the right ones. Slowly, I began to understand what it means to be a researcher: to stand before a mountain of knowledge, to frame a question from it, to pursue it relentlessly, to design your own method of inquiry, to test what you discover, and eventually, to piece it together into something coherent, meaningful, and shareable.

This insight into how researchers think and work is, I believe, what I will carry back most strongly from this fellowship. It has made me question how we approach information as teachers. We are often looking for answers. But this space taught me to get comfortable with uncertainty and look for questions instead.

The Archives revealed a layered and nuanced history of education in India through policies, textbooks, and the voices of those who shaped and challenged them. One of the most striking aspects of this journey was studying school textbook archives and seeing how they have evolved over time. These documents, simple as they may seem, are a reflection of our educational values, political shifts, and collective imagination. Understanding how a textbook is written and how it influences what and how we teach gave me an entirely new perspective on curriculum and its potential.

Along the way, I also found myself reflecting on the role of language in expressing thought. There were so many moments when I felt deeply moved, or thoughtful, or provoked by what I was reading, but I didn’t always have the vocabulary to express it. I realized how important it is to build a language for reflection, for critique, for curiosity. This experience has made me want to strengthen my academic and expressive vocabulary, not just to communicate better, but also to think more effectively.

Despite being physically exhausted at times (the days were long, packed, and intense), my mind felt alive in ways it hadn’t in a while. There’s something incredibly energizing about thinking deeply, about encountering new ideas, about puzzling over how to connect the dots. I felt a creative shift inside me from under-confidence and hesitation to a quiet confidence that comes from trying, failing, learning, and trying again. Journaling regularly helped me track this shift. Even though I often laughed at myself for forgetting how to spell the simplest of words on tired days, I found comfort in writing things down.

Nearing the end of the fellowship, I felt both a sense of sadness and fulfillment. It’s always bittersweet to come to the close of something transformative. But there was also a deep sense of pride in looking back and saying, “Look how far we’ve come.” The process of crafting our teaching ideas, presenting them, and waiting for feedback gave us a taste of accomplishment. It felt real and rooted in our own journeys.

One of the most enriching parts of the fellowship was the teachers I had the privilege of working alongside. Their stories, questions, and laughter added layers to my own reflections. We were all walking parallel but different paths, and the learning that came from these shared experiences was invaluable. I also want to thank the people at the Archives for their warmth, generosity, and kindness. They made the space inviting, collaborative, and full of possibility.

This fellowship has reminded me that education is not just about classroom instruction, it’s about context, history, questioning, and creativity. It’s about stepping away from the whiteboard to explore new tools, new spaces, and new questions. For any teacher looking to breathe fresh life into their practice, think deeply about their role, and rediscover the joy of learning, this is an incredible opportunity.

To experiment, to wonder, to feel overwhelmed, to try again, to reflect, and to grow, that is what this journey has been. I am leaving with more questions than I arrived with, and that, I believe, is a sign of meaningful learning.

*A three-week in-person fellowship at the Archives at NCBS, Bengaluru. It is aimed at middle and high school teachers. The focus is on using archival records (old documents, letters, photos, scientific history material) as tools for teaching.

Vaishnavi Bhat is an educator and curriculum designer working at the intersection of teacher training and hands-on learning. She can be reached at bhatvaishnavi93@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vaishnavi Bhat
From textbooks to policies, archival materials brought forth revelations that led to fresh classroom ideas. Vaishnavi Bhat reflects on the transformative NCBS summer fellowship.

item.title: Getting better at what we do
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

This month, I wish to share my own reflection on the question:

“How can a teacher keep getting better at her work?”

And the emphasis is on the phrase “keep getting”.

Yes, an occasional course or a workshop often injects us with new zeal, ideas, or even fresh content, and fuels our engines – for a while. But what then? How does one continue to cruise along with constant alertness to one’s own learning needs?

I stumbled upon a Ted talk which asked exactly this question.

One day, Dr Atul Gawande (a Boston-based American surgeon) realized that after a slow improvement in his medical practice for some years, he had plateaued. He wondered: Was there any way in which his practice could continue to improve? Those five years of medical school: were they sufficient to ensure that a doctor improved continuously for the rest of their career?

Of course not! Reflecting thus, he began to take a look at other professions. Sportsmen – even the very best – ALWAYS had a coach. Or did they? He discovered that coaching didn’t start until 1875 – when Yale hired a coach. In all Yale-Harvard football matches for the next three decades, Yale consistently won each and every match – except three! The result? Harvard hired a coach!

Was coaching restricted to sports, then, he wondered?

The violinist Itzhak Perelman declared that he definitely had a coach: his wife! She had studied music with him, and then chosen to give up her profession (as a violinist) in order to support her husband. She did this by watching each of his performances and giving him constructive feedback thereafter.

Could he, Dr Gawande, get a coach? He approached his former professor, now retired, with that request. Prof. Bob Osteen agreed. So in Dr Gawande’s next surgery, Bob Osteen was also present in the OT, quietly taking notes. After a very smooth surgery, Dr Gawande wasn’t expecting any earth-shattering feedback. But Bob shared “little things” like:

  • The light had swung out of the wound during the case.
    • Your elbow goes up in the air once in a while, instead of resting comfortably.

“Little things”? Definitely not! Especially for something as critical as surgery!

Clearly, his coach was of enormous help in:

  • Drawing his attention to “little things”.
  • Becoming his external eyes and ears.
  • Breaking down the fundamentals.
  • Providing a more accurate picture of HIS reality.
  • Coming up with a checklist.

This reinforced Dr G’s resolve to keep getting coached – and that set his learning curve on the rise again.

I now asked: If a doctor finds five years of medical school insufficient to ensure continuous improvement, how can a one-or-two-year B Ed for teachers ensure that their practice keeps getting better?

Was there a consistent coaching practice for teachers anywhere in the world?

My search led me to the age-old practice of Lesson Study in Japan. In this very interesting video of a grade II math teacher, Lesson Study is shown as consisting of these steps:

  1. The grade II math teacher sat with a group of colleagues and planned his lesson.
  2. Then, he transacted it – with his colleagues observing the class and taking notes.
  3. All of them then collectively analyzed the lesson.
  4. He incorporated their feedback and refined this lesson for future transactions.

Do watch this clip, as it will help you gain a better understanding.

My takeaways are:

  • Teachers who wish to continuously improve can do so through the process of Lesson Study.
  • For this, they need to find trusted educators who will observe their classroom transactions and offer constructive feedback.

And the cherry on the cake? A community of practice slowly builds!

Any takers?

References

  1. Caroline Chassels and Wayne Melville (2009) Canadian Journal of Education Vol. 32, No. 4 pp. 734-763.
  2. Brian Doig and Susie Groves (2011) Mathematics Teacher Education & Development Vol. 13.1 pp. 77-93.

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
How can teachers keep improving beyond initial training? Neeraja Raghavan draws lessons from Dr. Gawande and Japan’s Lesson Study to show the power of coaching and feedback.

item.title: Equitable assessments
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

As educators in India, we understand the incredible diversity that fills our classrooms. From bustling metropolitan spaces to those in quieter regions, our students bring with them a wealth of languages, cultures, and individual experiences. This vibrant mix, while enriching, also presents a unique challenge: how do we ensure that every student has a fair chance to demonstrate their understanding, particularly in subjects like mathematics?

The concept of equitable assessment offers a powerful lens through which we can address this challenge. It's crucial to understand that equitable assessment isn't about treating every student the same (equal assessment). Instead, it's about providing what each student needs at the right time and in the right way to truly showcase their learning (Kampen, 2020). Think of it like providing different-sized shoes to children – everyone gets a pair, but the size is tailored to fit each individual comfortably.

Why is equitable assessment so vital in middle school?

Our classrooms are incredibly diverse, encompassing variations in race, ethnicity, gender, sexual orientation, language, culture, religion, and varying mental and physical abilities (NEA, n.d.). In math classrooms, this diversity manifests in numerous ways:

  • Language diversity: While many students may be proficient in English or Hindi, some may be more comfortable learning and expressing themselves in their regional languages. Word problems, in particular, can become significant hurdles due to language complexity rather than mathematical understanding (Siegel, M.A., 2007).
  • Cultural context: The examples and scenarios we use in math problems might resonate more with some students than others based on their cultural experiences.
  • Learning style and pace: Students learn at different speeds and through different modalities. Some might grasp concepts quickly through abstract reasoning, while others may benefit from visual aids or hands-on activities.
  • Prior knowledge and access to resources: Students come with varying levels of prior mathematical knowledge and may have different levels of support and resources outside the classroom.
  • Specific learning needs: Some students may have learning disabilities or other challenges that require specific accommodations to participate fully in assessments.

Ignoring these differences can lead to assessments that inadvertently measure factors other than mathematical understanding, creating barriers for students and not providing a true picture of their abilities. This is where the principles of equitable assessment become indispensable.

Practical strategies

While various frameworks exist, the core idea revolves around adapting our teaching and assessment methods to meet the diverse needs of our learners (Siegel et al., 2008). Here are some practical strategies we can implement in middle school math classrooms:

Comprehension: making the language accessible

Ensuring students understand what is being asked of them is paramount. In a diverse classroom, this means paying close attention to the language used in assessments.

  • Strategy: Simplify language in word problems. Instead of complex sentence structures and unfamiliar vocabulary, use clear and concise language.
    • Example: Instead of: "The sum of the digits of a two-digit number is 9. If 27 is added to the number, the digits are reversed. Find the number."
    • Consider: "A two-digit number has digits that add up to 9. If you add 27 to this number, the order of the digits flips. What is the original number?" (Using simpler phrasing and breaking down the information).
  • Strategy: Provide visual aids. Diagrams, pictures, and bar models can help students visualize the problem and reduce reliance on strong language comprehension.
    • Example: For a problem involving ratios like "The ratio of boys to girls in a class is 3:2. If there are 12 boys, how many girls are there?", a bar model with 3 units representing boys and 2 units representing girls can visually demonstrate the relationship.

Elicitation: allowing diverse ways to show understanding

Designing tasks that allow all students to demonstrate their knowledge in ways that suit their strengths.
