  • Strategy: Offer varied response formats. Instead of solely relying on written answers, consider incorporating oral explanations, visual representations, or even short demonstrations where appropriate.
    • Example: When assessing understanding of angle properties, students could be asked to draw and label different types of angles or explain the relationship between complementary and supplementary angles verbally, instead of just writing their definitions.
  • Strategy: Break down complex problems. Large, multi-step problems can be overwhelming. Breaking them into smaller, manageable parts with clear instructions for each step can help students approach the problem systematically.
    • Example: When solving a problem involving profit and loss with multiple steps (calculating cost price, selling price, discount, profit percentage), provide guiding questions for each step: "What is the original cost price?", "What is the selling price after the discount?", "Calculate the profit amount.", "What is the profit percentage?"

Scaffolding: providing temporary support

Provide temporary support structures that help students access the assessment and demonstrate their learning. These supports are gradually removed as students become more proficient.

  • Strategy: Offer sentence starters and graphic organizers. These tools can provide a framework for students to organize their thoughts and express their understanding.
    • Example: For a problem requiring a written explanation of the Pythagorean theorem, provide sentence starters like: "In a right-angled triangle, the square of the...", or a graphic organizer with sections for identifying the hypotenuse, the other two sides, and the formula.
  • Strategy: Provide easier practice questions. Before a formal assessment, offering a set of simpler problems that focus on the core concepts can build confidence and allow students to solidify their understanding.
    • Example: Before assessing the concept of solving linear equations, provide practice problems involving single-step equations like x+5=10 or 2y=12.

Accommodation: adjusting for specific needs

Making necessary adjustments to the assessment process or environment to address the specific needs of students with disabilities or other learning challenges.

  • Strategy: Allow extra time. Students who need more time to process information or complete tasks should be provided with it.
  • Strategy: Provide a comfortable space. Ensuring a quiet and comfortable environment can reduce anxiety and help students focus better during assessments.
  • Strategy: Permit the use of tools. Depending on the student's needs, this might include calculators for complex computations, manipulatives for visual learners, or assistive technology for students with specific learning disabilities. Allowing the use of a bilingual dictionary can be crucial for students who are still developing their proficiency in the language of instruction.

Student agency and collaboration: empowering learners

Empowering students to take ownership of their learning and learn from each other.

  • Strategy: Encourage peer help. Collaborative learning can help students clarify their understanding through discussion and different perspectives.
    • Example: During a problem-solving activity, encourage students to work in pairs to understand the problem and discuss potential solution strategies before attempting it individually.
  • Strategy: Offer choices in assessment presentation. For projects or more open-ended tasks, allow students to choose how they want to present their findings (e.g., written report, presentation with diagrams, a short skit demonstrating the concept). This caters to different learning styles and strengths. For instance, when exploring data handling, some students might prefer to present their analysis in a written report with charts, while others might create a short presentation explaining their findings visually.

Conclusion

By intentionally incorporating these strategies, we can move towards creating more equitable learning environments in middle school math classrooms. This not only ensures fairer assessments but also fosters a sense of belonging and empowers all students to reach their full mathematical potential. The journey towards equitable assessment is an ongoing process. It requires continuous reflection, learning, and adaptation to the diverse needs of our students. By embracing these principles, we can create classrooms where every student feels seen, supported, and has the opportunity to shine in their mathematical journey.

References

  1. Kampen, M. (2020). 8 Powerful ways to promote equity in the classroom. https://www.prodigygame.com/main-en/blog/equity-in-the-classroom/
  2. National Education Association (NEA) website, Diversity Toolkit: http://www.nea.org/tools/diversity-toolkit-introduction.html
  3. Siegel, Marcelle. (2007). Striving for equitable classroom assessments for linguistic minorities: Strategies for and effects of revising life science items. Journal of Research in Science Teaching. 44. 864-881.10.1002/tea.20176.
  4. Siegel, M., Wisserh, C., & Halverson, K. (2008). A framework for equitable assessment. The Science Teacher, 44. http://people.uncw.edu/kubaskod/nc_teach/class_5_assessment/sounds_like_success_equitable_assessment.pdf
  5. Soika, B. (n.d.). Seven effective ways to promote equity in the classroom. USC Rossier-School Education. https://rossier.usc.edu/seven-effective-ways-to-promoteequity-in-the-classroom/

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
Not all students learn the same way or at the same pace. Monica Kochar writes about how math classrooms can ensure every student has a fair chance to show their understanding.

item.title: More about federal structures of governance – local self-government
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: We have been talking a lot about the complexities of federalism in the Indian constitution. But, I am not able to get a complete picture of the implications of federalism in India. I have also heard that our being colonized by the British and their ideas of democracy being imposed on us were the primary problems the Constituent Assembly was fighting. This is why I had raised the question of post-colonialism and how the Constitution was influenced by social and political realities of those times.

Anubhuti: Your question has two parts to it – the incoherence in the nature of federalism in India, and whether our constitution mirrors British ideas alone, or if we re-invoked institutions that existed in the pre-colonial times.

Our constitution is federal but veers towards unitarianism, which we have spoken about already. The powers to individual state governments are quite limited. Even financial powers are shared between the union and state governments. Another more complex issue is asymmetric federalism. So far, we have only been talking about the states and the Union of India. We also have other structural entities such as the union territories, which are directly ruled by the central government; some states also have special autonomy, like Jammu and Kashmir through article 370; which has now been revoked. There is the 5th and 6th Schedule in the Constitution, which focuses on regions that have a significantly high tribal population, who have specific primary concerns like laws that govern how land, forests, and mountains are managed, and even laws that govern marriages and inheritance. These Schedules are applied to various regions and populations in Andhra Pradesh, Meghalaya, Assam, Tripura, and Mizoram. There are other states that are fighting for addition to the Schedule, so that the local councils and governors have more authority on the laws and policies made in these regions.

Overall, the asymmetry in our federalism is by design. It has been introduced primarily to take care of the diversity in India, and to protect minorities who have special concerns.

Ira: Just when I thought this is as complex as democracy can get, you introduce me to more complexities I am not even aware of, living here in Bengaluru.

Anubhuti: These laws may not apply directly to us, but the principles on which the Constitution is based influences the way we see the laws that apply to us too. Local self-government is a very good example of what I am trying to say.

Remember you mentioned how the structures in India should be seen like a matrix, where power is distributed both vertically and horizontally? Power distribution does not end with bicameral legislatures. It goes all the way down to local institutions we interact with on a daily basis – municipal corporations, zilla parishads, village panchayats – all of which have different names and terms in different states.

The point I am making is that our Constitution is structured to take democratic power as close to each individual as possible.

Ira: That is interesting. Is this an example of what I was asking earlier – whether pre-colonial institutions were brought back after the British left? Or is there a complicated story here too?

Anubhuti: Ha ha ha. Yes there is. Let me begin with some entertaining quotes. The idea of a postcolonial society itself is complicated. It is a pejorative term, which indicates that once colonized, a society remains colonized because some sections of the society inherit the thinking of the colonizer, along with traces of thinking like the colonized. In India, a popular example is the respect we give to the English language and people who speak English – like you and me.

Manish Jain[i] says the British left India, but the notion of an ideal citizen they created in their civics textbooks, got converted into English-speaking middle-class Indians. But, at the same time, we rejected numerous ideas the British mentioned in the Government of India Act 1935 – including the principles of democracy based on freedom and equality, which they inherited from liberal thinkers like John Stuart Mill and Jeremy Bentham. Like Upendra Baxi[ii] eloquently said, there are always continuities and discontinuities in postcolonial societies.

Ira: Why does he say discontinuities? It seems strange to say that.

Anubhuti: Of course he doesn’t stop there. The statement he made, which I like is, “Constitutions are constructed, as moral autobiographies of “new” nations, promising a new future, vigorously disinvesting the colonial past.” He further argues that constitutionalism and colonialism are antithetical to each other. When we gave ourselves this Indian constitution in 1950, we created a rupture with the past. It is a moral statement we made when a new identity of India was created.

Ira: Moral autobiography of a nation! It is such a profound statement – calling the Constitution a moral document. This is interesting also because it draws a thin line between seeing the Constitution from two extreme viewpoints – a sacred document that cannot be altered and a pliant document that can be changed at will. Why is the local self-government an example of this rupture?

Anubhuti: India had had village councils – panchayats – for centuries. It even continued in some form during the times of the Mughal Empire. Of course, the council members were not elected like they are today, but there was a system in place at the village/town level.

The Montague-Chelmsford reforms in 1919 implemented the idea of local governments, but they were controlled by provincial governments. For them, a local government was not about devolution of power; rather they were seen as “an instrument of political and popular education” like Lord Ripon said in 1882. Basically, it was just a way to familiarize the Indian polity with the existence of governance structures and to provide them with access to those structures for their immediate needs. For the British, this was decentralization not devolution of power.

Ira: This sounds like a corruption of the idea Gandhi proposed in Hind Swaraj – that India should be a conglomeration of self-sufficient village republics. Was this seriously considered in the Constituent Assembly debates? It is a beautiful idea, isn’t it?

Anubhuti: Beautiful yes, but too idealistic some people thought. This resulted in prolonged debates in the Constituent Assembly, creating two sides. One side had people like K.T. Shah and Arun Chandra Guha who insisted that village panchayats should be the basic units of provincial governments and that they should be financially empowered as well.

On the other side were people, including Dr. Ambedkar, who insisted that individuals should be the basic unit of society and government, and not villages. He argued that villages survived numerous wars, dynasty changes, with little or no influence on the overall nation. He said, “…they have survived through all vicissitudes, may be a fact. But mere survival has no value. The question is on what plane they have survived. … villages are a sink of localism, a den of ignorance, narrow-mindedness, and communalism.”[iii] Obviously, the issue he was raising is that while Indian culture and traditions survived, it also carried regionalism, parochialism, ethnic divides, casteism, and other ills with little or no attention to the progress of our society. According to him, allowing that level of sovereignty for villages would be disastrous.

This debate reached an impasse and the compromise reached was to change this ideal of village panchayats and local self-government to the Directive Principles of State Policy (Article 40 of the Constitution).

Ira: Wasn’t this argument brought back later? We do have local self-governments now. This came as an amendment of the Constitution, didn’t it?

Anubhuti: Yes, that is another story. The popular logic brought by Prime Minister Rajiv Gandhi was that only 5000 to 6000 people who are members of the Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, and all state legislatures represented a population of more than 80 crores. If we introduced panchayats across the nation, we would have 700,000 representatives, which was more reasonable for a large population. He also recommended significant devolution of power to the panchayats. But these amendments were rejected in the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha. It was proposed again in different forms by two subsequent governments, but did not achieve success.

Finally, in 1992, these amendments with significant changes – mainly increasing control of the state governments on the panchayats and village councils – was presented to a Joint Parliamentary Committee and finally approved as the 72nd and 73rd amendment.

Ira: You mean the same debate the Constituent Assembly had on the sovereignty of local government continued in the 1990s! How was it resolved then?

Anubhuti: I am not so sure about the details, but it seems to me that the principle of subsidiarity was applied here. According to this principle, elected representatives who function at the level of a state or province are too distant from the daily realities of citizens. Dealing with infrastructural or documentation work with the state happens at very low levels. For example, to complain about bad roads, we have to go to the ward officer or municipal commissioner. Similarly, registration of properties, water supply, and other daily needs are taken care of by local bodies. Therefore, some amount of devolution of power becomes essential so that they can do the work for us. This also involves prioritizing, choosing solutions and these are issues that might have diverse opinions. If the local bodies have sufficient power, at least financially, they could independently do the work. They would also have to be people who represent the views of the population. This is a way to get closer to direct democracy.

But this model also makes a fundamental assumption that social solidarity exists. If some social cohesion did not exist already, it was bound to lead to issues of exclusion – some groups could be excluded based on caste, religion, or even ethnicity. So, some amount of control from the state government becomes essential to avoid such discriminations.

Ira: Which brings us back to Ambedkar’s argument of the risk of provincialism and localism. I need to know more about the nature of local government in India and how it is implemented. That will complete our discussion on federalism.

Anubhuti: There is a lot more to say about this; let’s take it up next time. It will also bring up questions of minorities protection, which is another salient feature of our constitution.

[i]Jain, M. (2005). Past and present in the curriculum. Mumbai: Economic and Political Weekly.

[ii]Baxi, Upendra. 2012. ‘Postcolonial Legality: A Postscript from India.’ Verfassung Und Recht in Übersee / Law and Politics in Africa, Asia and Latin America 45, no. 2 (2012): 178-94.

[iii]https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/04-nov-1948/#102890

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education and Curriculum theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. He can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
India’s local self-government has a long and complex history. Prakash Iyer writes about how federalism and postcolonial realities shaped the powers and functions of panchayats and municipal bodies.

item.title: A first step towards healing
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Radhika Chhaparia

At the heart of the book Where Does It Hurt? lies the inevitability of hurt as part of human experience and the significance of those who help alleviate it.

Sometimes, as this book conveys both emphatically and poetically, the journey towards healing begins with a conversation about hurt itself, set in motion by asking a simple yet potent question: Where does it hurt?

Penned in verse by Samina Mishra and illustrated by Allen Shaw, Where Does It Hurt? is a thought-provoking book which explores the challenging subject of hurt, pain, and related emotions experienced by children as they go through life. Part of the Hook Book series(1), it certainly lives up to its promise of offering something short and engaging for young readers, yet interesting enough for the older ones.

Written in the form of a conversation between child protagonists and adult characters, the text is primarily a series of episodes rather than a single story. Each episode presents readers with a situation in which a child is found struggling with a challenging experience and ensuing emotions. The entire narrative is constructed thoughtfully as it charts a progression in two aspects. First, the likelihood of children’s familiarity with the illustrated situations. Second, the complexity of emotions evoked through the experiences portrayed. In the first few pages, we encounter a little boy in physical pain after an accidental fall from his bicycle. Predictably, it leads to bruising, bleeding, and the loss of a tooth, whereas, in a later episode, a girl is found grappling with more complex emotions triggered by the loss of a loved one, which many readers would not have experienced yet and may find hard to empathize with. Each child character, no matter the circumstance, meets someone who pops the thoughtful question, “Where does it hurt?” kick-starting their journey towards healing. The text stays authentic to real-life experiences of unfortunate and emotionally challenging events and subtly conveys that sometimes our feelings don’t disappear quickly or perhaps even completely. This aspect is conveyed through a sense of uncertainty which creeps into the narrative as it progresses towards more challenging life circumstances. After all, the immediacy and certainty with which a particular hurt can be assuaged not only depends on the help one receives but also on how deep it hurts in the first place. Sometimes, the length of time that has passed before one finally meets a caring soul can also impact our healing journey. Another contributing factor would be our own resilience. Finally, the closing lines in the book nudge all readers to recall a similar episode from their lives and inquire about what particularly occurred for them when they found themselves on either side of the question ‘where does it hurt?’ Did they feel relieved and cared for? Did they hesitate in asking another person about the reason behind their pain? Was it easy to talk about their feelings when someone reached out to them? Or did the question cause them to go even further inside their cocoon? Despite the seriousness of the topic, the overall tone of the book remains uplifting and the accompanying images in the book add towards this. Given that the book is aimed at young readers, it is befitting that each episode is resolved on a hopeful note and all characters in the story receive help from someone. In my view, multiple short plotlines, diverse settings and the variety of characters employed in the book will also help young readers relate to and grasp the familiar yet wide-ranging experience of hurt. The episodic format is also likely to naturally excite young readers to share similar anecdotes from their lives.

Generally, the articulation of a hurtful experience is difficult for most of us, but it can be particularly challenging for children. On the one hand, children could find their feelings overwhelming and consequently struggle with articulation. On the other, they could find themselves in the company of adults preoccupied with overcoming life circumstances such as chronic poverty, unemployment, violence, etc., causing them to deliberately hold back from communicating with adults. Understandably, children’s helplessness in talking about their pain is often expressed in non-verbal ways such as tears, withdrawal from others, sadness, irritability, and tantrums. Their struggle to articulate difficult experiences is well-portrayed in Allen Shaw’s illustrations and in the recurrence of the question ‘Where does it hurt?’ Not only does his artwork help us visualize what is expressed in words, it also personifies hurt and does so with a touch of humour. Along with other human characters there is also the quintessential villain Hurt, unmissable in its red suit and devilish horns. Hurt was a real surprise for me when I first read the book.

Brimming with emotions akin to other characters in the story, Hurt is seen afflicting one child after another in its indefatigable quest to cause pain, and is eventually driven away from various scenes of crime by those who care. What is outstanding about the images is that, much like the words, they too give rise to further thoughts and questions: Do I identify with the character Hurt? Who is responsible for my feelings? Could I blame someone for my hurt? How do I feel once I recognize that I hurt someone? Did I find any pleasure in it? Did I hurt someone unintentionally, and if not, what motivated me? Do I have the courage to ask someone I hurt, ‘Where does it hurt?’ or do I find it easier to silently walk away?

Further questions that may arise in the minds of mature readers are: Was I hurt intentionally, or is my pain caused by unintended consequences, or a mere accident? Does the aforementioned consideration alter the way I now experience the event?

In my opinion, this short and well-illustrated book is remarkable in its ability to offer a sophisticated and easy to understand exploration of difficult life experiences and complex human emotions to readers as young as five. A mix of settings and the vibrant character Hurt make the book somewhat light-hearted even though its theme could easily weigh down any reader and I will encourage teachers to approach the book in a similar spirit when reading it with children.

In terms of accessibility of language and presentation of ideas, the book seems suitable for independent reading, group-reading, and a read-aloud with children of ages five and above. However, given the complexity of the theme, my recommendation would be to read it in a group-setting along with an adult when sharing the book with children below the age of 10. The two short exercises included at the end of the book seem appropriate for its youngest readers: each provides a space for personal reflection in the context of their own experience of hurt and pain. Teachers are encouraged to come up with similar exercises for older children. An art-based activity in which children are encouraged to express similar anecdotes through drawings could also be undertaken.

With senior-school children, the book appears to carry the potential of being a playful conversation starter to explore the significance of creating communities of care in their particular socio-cultural context, and discuss their notions of care and wellbeing. Hopefully, teachers will not shy away from taking this book to older children. For parents, this book serves as an important, accessible, and affordable guide on the topic. The universality of the theme would likely make it possible to facilitate book discussions with groups of diverse individuals and perhaps even intergenerational dialogues on the topic. Like the tip of an iceberg, Where Does It Hurt? points to further questions to reflect on for all readers irrespective of their age: Are certain painful situations avoidable, like war, bombing and displacement of communities? What holds me back from asking ‘where does it hurt?’ even when I’m aware of another's pain? Do I avoid interacting with those who appear to be gripped by sadness and loneliness?, and many more. Adult readers could also consider listening to the ‘On Being’ podcast with Ruby Sales(2) hosted by Krista Trippett, from which this book draws its inspiration.

As a final thought, I think an illustration or two of a role reversal wherein a child poses the question ‘where does it hurt?’ to an adult would’ve added another interesting layer to the book. Similarly, a child posing the question to another child. Nevertheless, nothing stops a keen reader of this book and of life to explore such aspects of human relationships during a book discussion.

(1) Hook Book series, published by Duckbill (an imprint of Penguin Random House India) and designed by Priya Kuriyan, consists of chapter books in English for young readers. They primarily attempt to hook readers to stories which are fun, well-illustrated and short but also have an interesting story to tell, even for an older reader. Books in this series come with a short exercise or two to deepen a reader’s engagement.

(2) Ruby Nell Sales is an African-American social justice activist, scholar, and public theologian.

Radhika Chhaparia has obtained an MA in Education from Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. During the academic years 2020-2023 she worked at Center For Learning (CFL) school, Bengaluru. She can be reached at radhika.chhaparia16_mae@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Radhika Chhaparia
Where Does It Hurt? is a gentle, poetic book by Samina Mishra and Allen Shaw that helps young readers understand hurt, empathy, and the journey towards healing. Check out Radhika Chhaparia’s review of the book.

item.title: Hello, sweetness!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sujata C

India has the dubious distinction of being the world capital for diabetes. To create awareness of the risks of high consumption of sugar among school children, the government has mandated the installation of a ‘Sugar Board’ in CBSE schools that displays the recommended intake of sugar and the amount of sugar in foods that children love.

This is an attempt to increase nutritional awareness among children and promote preventive healthcare. Hence, a project on sugar will further push children to learn everything about sugar – the one thing that is everyone’s favourite and nobody’s requirement. This project can be done with students of all classes and scaled to their level of understanding. It cuts across subjects.

So why do we love sugar so much? Science says that eating sugar releases endorphins, which are mood boosting hormones that create pleasurable sensations. The feel-good sensation makes you want to eat it more and more. But over-stimulation can lead to addictive behaviour, which sugar has a potential for. The brain’s sensitivity to the feel-good hormones decreases with over exposure and the body needs higher doses of sugar to achieve the same effect. After eating sweets or sugar, the body experiences a sugar high which is very enjoyable and this is followed by a crash when the endorphin levels drop and the body experiences something similar to withdrawal symptoms that are characterized by irritability, mood swings, and cravings. While this can be explained to students of higher classes you will have to start with the basics for the younger children.

Sugar and health: Sugar is called white poison, because it provides empty calories, no nutrients and brings health risks. Since sugar can be addictive, over consumption leads to various health conditions such as obesity, diabetes, and tooth decay. Sugar also reduces immunity and invites a host of ailments by feeding the harmful bacteria. Packaged foods often contain sugar as it enhances taste. Tomato ketchup, yogurt, salad dressing, mayonnaise, all contain sugar. You will find the words ‘added sugar’ on the package – this is called hidden sugar and can come under different names such as fructose, maltose, dextrose, etc. Free sugar, on the other hand, is the white crystal sugar we add to milk, tea, coffee, sweets, kheer, cookies, cakes, etc.

Make a list of food items that contain hidden sugar.

Talk: Ask your class to speak to someone in their family or extended circle who suffers from diabetes – Type 1 or 2.

WHO recommends that only 10% of the energy intake for both adults and children can come from free sugar. For additional health benefits, less than 5% is recommended. But doctors say it is advisable to consume as little sugar as possible and even eliminate it entirely if possible. A baby’s diet from conception to two years (the first 1000 days of a child) must be sugarfree, doctors say and many baby food brands are complying with this rule. How much sugar are you consuming every day? Calculate. Track it for three days – make a note in a diary.

Compare: Make a table to compare the sugar content of coke vs homemade lassi and lemon juice.

Schools now must display a Sugar Board which looks like this:

Language: Examine the roots of the word sugar. Ikshu is Sanskrit for sugarcane. Study the similarity between the words, sucre, saccharine, shakkar, and zuker.

Why is sugar called chini in Hindi? Find out.

Make a sentence using sweetness as a metaphor, and idioms such as sweet talk, sugar-coated, etc.

Write a poem/limerick/haiku on sugar.

Botany: Where does sugar come from? Discuss the plant, a grass native to New Guinea islands in the Pacific Ocean, and how it thrives in hot and humid climates. Sacchurumrobustum is the wild species of sugarcane, which was domesticated and hybridized through agricultural techniques. How is the sugarcane different from other grasses? Make a chart to explain.

Map work: Locate sugar producing nations on a map.

Geography: Brazil is the world’s largest producer of sugarcane and India holds the second position. Roughly 80% of India’s sugarcane is produced in three states: Uttar Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Karnataka. Older students can be asked to find out: How much sugarcane does India produce? How much land is under sugar cultivation – in the world and in India? Which state in India is the highest producer of sugarcane? Which is the sugarcane harvest season? Ask how many students have actually seen a sugarcane field.

Commerce: Sugarcane is a major cash crop for India, supporting crores of Indians. Find out the size of the sugar industry in India, foreign exchange earnings, and related government policies. Present this information on a chart. Asia’s largest sugar factory used to be the Nizam Sugar Factory in Nizamabad, Telengana. Find out the story behind its rise and fall.

‘Sugar barons’ are influential families that own/control the sugar manufacturing businesses. They are often politically connected. Find out names of popular sugar barons of India.

The Government of India has recently passed an order approving the addition of 20% ethanol to petrol. Ethanol is a by-product of sugar production and is mixed with gasoline to reduce carbon emission. Find out how this will impact older vehicles and why ethanol is a cleaner burning alternative to fossil fuels.

Did You Know: Mauritius has a sugar museum to familiarize visitors with sugar, its production, history, culture, and economy since sugar is a major industry there. Does any other nation have a sugar museum? Find out.

Chemistry/Science: How is crystal sugar made? Familiarize the class with the process. Discuss the role of sulphur in refining sugar. Why is sulphur-free sugar popular among the health conscious? What harm does sulphur cause to the body?

Talk about the different types of sugarcane based products: misri, khand, jaggery, brown sugar, and white sugar.

Explain, with the help of videos, how sugar is made, starting with the process of harvesting, milling, etc. If possible arrange a visit to a sugar factory. Discuss how sugarcane juice is processed to make the tabletop sugar we all know. C6H12O6 is the glucose molecule, whereas a sugar molecule is C12H22O11 – glucose and fructose bonded together.

Find out how sugar is broken down in the body? What is the difference between natural sugars (in fruits/honey) and added sugars? Write a report. Why do we feel heavy after eating dessert? Find out.

Experiment: Test for sugar content in common drinks with Benedict’s solution.

Cooking: Make sugar syrup – 1 string, 2 string, and 4 string consistency. Prepare caramel syrup.

Scientist Dr. Janaki Ammal is one lady every Indian must know about. She was a trailblazer, the first botanist of the nation, known for her research on sugarcane hybrids and brinjal. She is credited with hybridizing the sweetest variant of sugarcane (Saccharum officianarum) for Indian soil conditions. This eventually led to India becoming the world’s second largest sugarcane producer in the world.

History: Ancient India was not only growing sugarcane as early as the Vedic period, but had also pioneered sugar extraction and crystallization techniques long before the rest of the world. This knowledge travelled westward via trade and conquest, forming the foundation of the global sugar industry. Archaeo-botanical findings of sugarcane phytoliths in ancient Indian sites support early cultivation. It spread to the rest of the world through human migration and trade.

Sugarcane finds mention in Atharva Veda and CharakSamhita, the foundational Ayurvedic textbook on traditional Indian medicine. The book describes sugarcane as a natural diuretic, sweet, and wholesome substance with cooling properties. It qualifies sugarcane juice obtained from chewing as superior. Prepare a note on sugarcane in CharakSamhita.

Greek historian Nearchus (4th century BCE), accompanying Alexander the Great to India, referred to sugarcane as, "A reed in India that brings forth honey without the help of bees." New Guinea and the Caribbean islands also grew sugar in the olden days.

The history of sugar is tainted with the sweat and tears of slavery. When people discovered how sugar enhances the taste of food, the demand for sugar surged unimaginably. To cater to this, sugarcane production was scaled up to massive levels. The need for farm hands was met through trans-Atlantic slave trade. People from Africa were brought to the Caribbean islands, where massive sugar plantations were grown. Slave based plantation economy thus came into being and with it the untold stories of the miserable lives of the slaves. With mechanization of agriculture, this dark chapter in the history of sugar was closed. Older students can prepare a note on Sugarcane and Slavery – The Bitter Truth.

Prepare a timeline of sugar through the ages.

Culinary arts: Who doesn’t love freshly pressed sugarcane juice with a squeeze of lemon and a dash of ginger on a hot summer afternoon? Chewing on freshly cut sugarcane stalks and slurping up the sweet juice is a cherished childhood memory. Traditionally, sugar candy dolls were given during various festivals like Sankranti and Diwali, both in North and South India.

Batasha is a traditional Indian aerated sugar candy, often used as prasad in temples and during festivals in North India. It is also consumed as a sweet treat and can be found in various shapes and colours. Watch a video to see how batasha is made.

Environment: The dry pithy residue that remains after the extraction of juice from sugarcane is called bagasse. Traditionally, majority of sugar factories reused the bagasse as biofuel to generate steam and electricity for their own operations. It continues to be a source of biofuel but in a limited way. Bagasse is also a green and eco-friendly substitute for plastic and is used to make packaging material, cutlery, cups, etc. Innovative uses have been devised for bagasse.

  • In agriculture as organic matter to improve soil health.
  • A low-cost medium for mushroom cultivation.
  • Used as a base to make biodegradable, eco-friendly paper, cutlery, cups, plates, etc.

Sugar substitute: With diabetes becoming a global pandemic, many sugar substitutes have entered the market, among these are stevia, monk fruit, and xylitol.

  • Stevia is a herb with sweet tasting leaves and can be grown in a pot.
  • Monk fruit is native to South China and not easily accessible in India.
  • Xylitol is a sugar alcohol present in most plant material. Humans and animals also produce trace quantities of xylitol while breaking down carbohydrates. It is extracted from plants and made available in crystals for use as an artificial sweetener.

Prepare a chart on sugar substitutes and their uses.

Spiritual lessons from sugarcane: Sugarcane is often used as a metaphor for something that may seem tough on the outside but contains hidden sweetness within. To enjoy its juice, the cane must be crushed – symbolizing the effort needed to extract value or meaning from something. Similarly, the wisdom in sacred scriptures cannot be truly appreciated until it is simplified and made accessible. A cold press is needed to extract juice from sugarcane, just as a guru is needed to extract and convey the essence of knowledge.

In this metaphor, sweetness refers to knowledge, and the guru is the one who helps us access and savour it. The soul naturally longs for this sweetness – the clarity, depth, and joy that true knowledge brings – and it is through the guru’s guidance that the soul is able to experience it.

To conclude the project on a philosophical note, reflect on the concept of sweetness in thought, word and action – a principle that encourages us to express gentleness, kindness, and grace in everyday life, allowing the sweetness of inner wisdom to flow outwardly in all that we do.

Sujata C is an independent writer, editor and lifelong learner. She can be reached at sujata117@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sujata C
Why do we love sugar? Sujata C’s multidisciplinary project on sugar delves into different aspects of sugar – science, history, culture, and health – helping students to question their daily habits and understand its significance.

item.title: Are kids' newspapers obsolete in the era of doomscrolling?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sanjhee Gianchandani

A recent interaction between the Chief Minister of Uttar Pradesh, Yogi Adityanath, and the toppers of class 10 and 12 in Lucknow revealed two interesting things – he asked them if they read newspapers which first revealed that not only them, but students in classes 8 and 9 studying in prestigious English-medium schools across the country also do not read the newspaper but might skim through the headlines once in a while. Second, even if they read the newspapers, they focus on the puzzles. Let’s try to investigate the reasons behind this.

A newspaper is defined as a publication that is usually issued daily, weekly, or at other regular times and provides news, views, features, and other information of public interest. Since the blessed newspaper has forerunners dating back to 17th century England, a majority of people believe that it is the Bible of literary and political opinion and the most reliable source of news and information, providing comprehensive coverage of both local events and global issues. But today, when we are sitting on the brink of the 22nd century in a world that is fast-paced and technology-driven, is reading the printed newspaper still relevant if the only objective is to remain abreast with current affairs? We all know the answer: it is unequivocally, ‘no’.

Both school-going children and the adults in their ecosystems have increasingly moved away from reading newspapers, opting instead to stay informed through online news or by receiving headline updates via news apps on their phones. Here are some findings from an independent research survey[*] conducted with 200 Gen Z individuals, from both rural and urban areas, regarding their preferred methods for reading the news every morning.

This could be the case for several reasons:

  1. Accessibility: There are apps available for almost all newspapers in India, be it The Times of India, The Hindustan Times, The Hindu or even regional newspapers such as Punjab Kesari or Asomiya Pratidin. News on these apps can be read conveniently anytime, anywhere, unlike physical copies, which can be consumed only at specific locations.
  2. Cost-effectiveness: Producing digital content is generally cheaper than print media. This allows digital media platforms to offer more content at lower or no cost to readers. Therefore, we can see a post-pandemic shift to relying on online news portals. Sumitra Rawat, whose three daughters are studying in classes 6, 8, and 10 in a local school, admitted, “In the pandemic, my husband's salary was reduced and prices went up. We had to cut corners, and we stopped buying newspapers.”
  3. Interactivity: We must not forget that our students belong to Gen Alpha and now Gen Beta. They are used to dynamic reading on social media, which has an array of interactive features such as videos, hyperlinks, sharing options, buttons for reactions, and comment sections. This makes the reading of updates a much more engaging activity when compared to the static nature of the printed newspaper.
  4. Personalization: Since the advent of AI, it is very easy to access content that is tailored to one’s preferences. This applies to news items as well. The articles we engage with online can generate similar ones and satiate our topics of interest, whereas in print media, we have to go with a one-size-fits-all approach.
  5. Environmental impact: On the surface, digital media is more environmentally friendly and produces a less-intensive carbon footprint as compared to their print counterparts. The carbon footprint of a newspaper depends on a host of factors – production, printing and transport, whether the consumer recycles it after use, and so on. According to a report published in The Guardian: At the highest end of the spectrum, purchasing seven bulky papers each week, all of them printed on typical paper stock and ultimately sent to landfill, could create as much as a tonne of CO2 emissions each year. That's comparable to two short-haul flights!

While it is established that adults have shifted to accessing news digitally, what options do students in India have? Mainstream newspapers that adults used to read exhaustively contain mature and sensitive content, which is not suitable for children. These newspapers often cover topics such as politics, crime, global conflicts, or economic issues, which may be irrelevant to the day-to-day lives of school-going children. Moreover, they may begin to feel anxious and encumbered after reading these narratives. Also, the tone of writing is often caustic, serious, dry, and incisive, which may not sit well with young minds who are accustomed to reading engaging, crisp, and positive writing in their school textbooks and even their leisure reading. This is where one would expect children’s newspapers to step in. By way of design, these should provide a selection of news items that are appropriate for children. Articles need to be rewritten in a way that resonates with children's perspectives, ensuring the content is relatable and accessible. The information should be presented in a balanced, unbiased manner, free from complex jargon or controversial themes.

Providing a mix of educational, entertaining, and culturally relevant topics can help young readers understand the world around them in a way that fosters curiosity while promoting critical thinking and empathy. Newspapers must maintain their core purpose of ‘informing’ readers, rather than turning them into mere supplementary activity books. They should prioritize delivering relevant, factual, and timely news, helping children develop their unique worldviews. While activities may be included, they should never dwarf the primary goal of news reporting – arming young readers with knowledge, nurturing critical thinking, and promoting awareness of current events.

Let’s look at a few children’s newspapers available in India with their advantages and disadvantages listed for a comparative analysis.

It does not take a genius educator to realize the value addition made by effective newspapers in a classroom setting. They help in developing reading sub-skills, utilizing zero periods, pointing out printing mistakes, differentiating between facts and opinions, scurrying for specific news items, writing opinion pieces and news reports based on headlines, organizing debates on hot topics, and a plethora of other activities. But the larger question we must ask ourselves is how important it is for us to know what constitutes “news” and whether we need to know everything that is termed as news these days.

Trust in news is falling, with much of the public, especially younger age groups, increasingly turning away from it. The 2022 Reuters Institute Digital News Report found that consumption of traditional news media declined in nearly all the countries surveyed, with online and social consumption of news not making up for it. The proportion of news consumers who say they "avoid news" has risen sharply across countries, with the report describing the phenomenon as "selective avoidance". Reasons for this aversion include being put off by the repetitiveness of the news agenda, being worn out by the news, and not trusting it. So, when this shift is happening, it is happening for kids too. Let’s consider other ways of building reading skills, avoiding excessive screen time, enhancing their levels of curiosity, and engaging in meaningful discussions rather than relying solely on newspapers to do so.

References:

  • https://www.newindianexpress.com/states/kerala/2009/Aug/23/newspaper-reading-essential-for-teachers-studen-79703.html
  • https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/nagpur/teachers-inculcating-reading-habits-among-rural-students-through-newspapers/articleshow/81740481.cms
  • https://www.deccanherald.com/india/children-do-not-read-newspapers-books-anymore-say-up-teachers-1120586.html
  • https://www.theguardian.com/environment/green-living-blog/2010/nov/04/carbon-footprint-newspaper
  • chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/https://www.universalai.in/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/FROM-PRINT-TO-PIXEL_-ANALYSIS-OF-PERCEPTION-OF-GEN-%E2%80%93-Z-TOWARDS-SUSTAINABILITY-IN-PRINT-AND-ONLINE-NEWS-FORMATS-KRI160622.pdf

[*]Tandon Vijay, Nema Devanshi, and Saini Ajay Kumar (July 2023) From Print to Pixel: Analysis of Perception of Gen Z Towards Sustainability in Print and Online News Formats , Korea Review Of International Studies, Volume 16 (Special Issue 06) Page 113-126. https://www.universalai.in/wp-content/uploads/2020/03/FROM-PRINT-TO-PIXEL_-ANALYSIS-OF-PERCEPTION-OF-GEN-%E2%80%93-Z-TOWARDS-SUSTAINABILITY-IN-PRINT-AND-ONLINE-NEWS-FORMATS-KRI160622.pdf

Sanjhee Gianchandani works as an English language curriculum designer and editor. She holds a Master’s degree in English from Lady Shri Ram College for Women, University of Delhi and a CELTA from the University of Cambridge. Her rich experience ranges from working as an English language assessment specialist to being a Writing/Speaking examiner for various international examinations to leading examiner/marker cohorts and finally managing content for the school segment. She has authored three series: Grammar Sparkle, Let’s Listen and Speak, and Epic English Grammar. Additionally, she is empanelled as a consultant editor with various publishing houses across the country. She has edited over 100 books ranging from academic to fiction, non-fiction, poetry, and children’s writing. She is passionate about writing and her articles on ELT pedagogy and learning strategies have been published in several educational magazines, journals, and blogs. She can be reached at sanjheegianchandani28@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sanjhee Gianchandani
From apps to headlines on phones, traditional kids’ newspapers seem irrelevant in 2025. Sanjhee Gianchandani delves into what children are reading today and how we can help kids build news literacy while balancing screen time.

item.title: Deeper into the tamarind grove
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat

Sitting under the tamarind trees, the students started discussing and writing. After some time, Prabhakar asked them, “I hope you have written down the names of the trees you know. Can you read them out?”

The students started shouting out the names one by one – mango, jackfruit, jamun, neem, coconut, arecanut, millingtonia, jacaranda, banyan, palm, champak, sandalwood, teak, gooseberry….

Prabhakar said, “Ok, collectively this is not more than 20 names. Some of them, like the coconut, grow on cultivated crop land. We are discussing forest trees.”

I explained, “The Kodagu district of Karnataka has evergreen forests with high mountain peaks and valleys. Kodagu has more than 200 sacred groves.” I listed the names of 87 different types of trees in 21 devarakadus, in addition to small plants and climbers. “The Neerulli Bana stretches 532 hectares. Imagine the extent of the forest! I visited Vanabhadra Kali grove, one of the largest devarakadus. As the name suggests, Bhadra Kali is the goddess guarding that forest. It was spread over 32 hectares. In Kodemangalore, a Bombax ceiba (silk cotton) tree has been found to have a girth of 10m. Sacred groves are home to many endangered flora and fauna. For example, Canarium strictum (dhoop tree) is categorized as a threatened species under the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN). It is on the Red List. Similarly, Malabar civet (Civet cat), endemic to the Western Ghats, is also found in these groves. These forests are close to wildlife reserves. You must have heard about the Nagarhole Tiger Reserve. The deverakadus in Kodagu are part of it. The temples in devarakadu are not always pukka buildings. It may just be a stone, a tree, or sometimes a few terracotta artifacts inside a temple-like structure. However, nowadays you do see contemporary temple structures and proper pathways to some of these temples inside the groves.”

One of the students said, “I am from Kerala, Iringolekavu is near my village. I had been there with my grandfather during the summer holidays. Here is a photo.”

Prabhakar called everyone to attention, “Students, there is a lot more to see here. Let us have some food and then move to the other side of the grove.”

The local guard, Muniyappa, came and cautioned us, “Here, monkeys will trouble you. You'd better sit near the temple, where there is a drinking water tap too.”

We all crossed the road and sat under the peepal tree by the side of the temple. The college students and our Backyard Group sat together to enjoy food.

Vivan said, “Monkey is staring from a distance, if it comes here, what to do?”

“Monkeys and dogs get scared if they see someone carrying a stick. I am here, your guard. I will chase them away,” Muniyappa said.

“Since when have you been guarding this grove?” Anand asked.

“Must be from the past 50-60 years, do not remember, I live nearby,” Muniyappa said.

“What do you do with these many tamarind trees? What is the quantity of fruits you collect in a year?” Shalini asked.

“The fruits ripen in the winter season. After that, they will be collected in March and April by the villagers. The collection is auctioned, and the income will be shared among the collectors,” Muniyappa said.

“It is good income for the villagers, is it not?”a student asked.

“Yes, do you think humans will conserve anything if they do not find any monetary benefit out of it?” Prabhakar asked.

Sujatha said,“I agree with you sir. Good thought by our ancestors, they developed such a treasure for the future population.”

“So, overall, who manages the land? The forest department?” a student asked.

“There you see the signboard installed by the State Biodiversity Board. There is a National Biodiversity Authority (NBA) in India. It is a statutory autonomous body under the Ministry of Environment, Forests, and Climate Change, Government of India, established in 2003. Under that, at the state level, there are State Biodiversity Boards,” Prabhakar said.

“Can we visit the State Biodiversity Board to learn about their activities?” a student asked.

“Possible. We can also be involved in their work,” I replied.

“That’s great, how do we approach them and what kind of work can we be involved in?” a student asked.

“If you want to understand how you can help, let us do an activity. Start by dividing yourselves into groups. Say the numbers 1, 2, 3, and 4. Those who said 1 will be in group ‘one’. Those who said 2 will be grouped in ‘two.’ Got it?” I instructed.

Prabhakar said, “Each group should go in a different direction. Write down the names of plants, animals, and insects you see. If you don’t know the name, take a picture, or draw a diagram to find out later. You should maintain complete silence when you walk, otherwise, insects and birds will get disturbed and fly away. We will meet back here in half an hour.”

Sujatha and Madhavi also went with the groups. After half an hour, all groups returned and started showing the pictures they had taken – butterflies, ant nests, wasps, flowers, grass, etc. The college students were able to identify the plants and birds. Prabhakar helped them identify the butterflies. There were photos of a brown butterfly sitting on the Tridax flower, a Pansy on a milkweed plant, and an ant nest on a tree. The nest was built by bringing the leaves of the tree together. The Backyard Group was happy to see and listen to this information given by the students.

“See the wasp nest. It made a perfect geometry of cells to lay eggs. The bracket-tailed bird is ‘Drango,’ and the noisy ‘Babbler,’” Shalini exclaimed with excitement.

Madhavi showed me a picture, “What are these green net-like threads spread on the bushes? And what about the pink flowers on the other side?”

I called everyone, “Come here and see this picture. Can you guess what this green colour thread spread over this bush is?”

“Some plant roots hanging?” someone asked in a feeble voice. Another student said, “Some waste material thrown on the bushes?”

“Is it some kind of plant growing on the bushes?” Anand asked.

“Good answer. It is a parasitic plant commonly known as Dodder or Cuscuta. It can be identified by its thin stem. The leaves are reduced to minute scales. The Dodder flower ranges from white to pink or cream colours. Some flowers bloom in early summer, others later. Dodder seeds are minute and sprout at or near the surface of the soil. Seed germination can occur without a host plant, but it should reach a green plant quickly. It reaches nearby plants by following chemosensory clues or chemical signals sent out from the host plants,” I explained.

A student replied, “The pink flower plant is Ipomoea, which is a climber. It has bulbous roots. I saw one on the roadside on my way home.”

“You all identified many small plants, trees, birds, and wasps in a short time and in a small area. Consider your village or town, and the numbers of plants and animals you will find. Proper documentation is required to understand the biodiversity of a place. Apart from this, there may be a river, a pond, or a hillock that hosts different types of plants and animals. To take up a conservation program or plan developmental activities, it is necessary to have a record of the biotic and abiotic components of the place. To do this, people living in a particular place need to prepare a biodiversity register. At the state level, the State Biodiversity Board facilitates the preparation of People's Biodiversity Registers (PBR). Your college can participate in this program. You can help the villagers document and spread awareness on the importance of documenting biodiversity,” I explained.

“Sir, when can we be involved in PBR preparation, and whom do we approach?” the students asked.

Prabhakar replied, “We need to enquire with the Board, but before that, you must know more about biodiversity and conservation.”

Suggested activities

  1. Name the avenue side trees near your school.
  2. Give examples of chemosensory receptors.

References

  • https://www.isec.ac.in/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/PB-31-Sacred-Groves-in-India_Final.pdf
  • https://kodavaclan.com/images/ebook/Preservation_of_Devara_Kadu_in_Kodagu_District.pdf
  • https://apistaging.iucnredlist.org/species/117894243/117894247
  • http://www.sacon.in/wp-content/uploads/2015/06/Kumara/2013-PR-115-BRT_SmallCarnivore_Report_final.pdf

Nandini Dholepat is a botanist who has been involved in teaching and research for more than 25 years. Currently, she is engaged as a consultant for Intellectual Property Rights for Karnataka State Council for Science and Technology, Bengaluru, and is a visiting faculty member at RIWATCH, Roing, Arunachal Pradesh. She can be reached at nandinidholepat@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Nandini Dholepat
The group continues to explore Kodagu’s sacred forests to observe the plants and wildlife. Nandini Dholepat also writes about how documenting biodiversity can shape conservation efforts.

item.title: The illusion of grandeur: prioritizing infrastructure over teachers
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prachi Lahiri

As I was driving down the road, my eyes fell on a huge hoarding by the side of a busy road in Bengaluru. It was an advertisement for a school. It proudly presented an Olympic size swimming pool, a gym, sprawling acres of land, digital boards, and lush green playgrounds. There was no mention of the teachers. It was completely silent about the teachers’ qualifications, their experiences, and their achievements. There was no mention of the teacher-student ratio either. The advertisement could easily be mistaken for that of an apartment complex or resort.

In the race to attract admissions and emerge as "top" educational institutions, many schools in India have begun to prioritize infrastructure and aesthetics over the very heart of education – the quality of teaching. While these features create an impressive façade for parents who seek the best for their children, they often mask the underlying deficiencies in the core of educational quality – most notably, the lack of focus on teacher training, experience of the teachers, their achievements, and effective student-teacher engagement.

Education in India is becoming increasingly commodified. Private schools mostly operate like businesses, where parents are the customers and students, the products. Schools invest heavily in infrastructure to lure the ‘customers’. Acres of land, air-conditioned auditoriums, and branded school buses with GPS trackers have become the norm. The emphasis is on selling a status symbol rather than providing a strong academic foundation. For example, rather than promoting the importance of simple and healthy home-cooked meals, these advertisements glorify a cafeteria with a global cuisine.

Such advertisements distract young parents from what truly matters. There is little discussion about the values and vision that the institution follows, or the qualifications and training of the faculty members, or the teaching methodologies employed by the institution. The message is clear: parents are expected to be wowed by the outer shell and not probe into the academic core.

Though there is a constant need for all other professionals to upgrade their skills, a bachelor's in education done decades ago is well accepted for a schoolteacher. The training programmes made available by the government are neither interactive, nor engaging, nor beneficial to the teacher in her classroom.

The recruitment and training of teachers in many Indian schools lacks standardization and rigor. Often, they are expected to teach across multiple subjects or grade levels. Many private schools, especially the budget ones, hire fresh graduates with little to no training, because they are affordable. There’s little investment in continuous professional development or exposure to new teaching techniques. As a result, teaching becomes more about rote learning and syllabus completion rather than nurturing critical thinking or creativity.

When was the last time a school proudly advertised the qualifications or accomplishments of its teachers on a billboard or brochure? The experience of a teacher who has produced national toppers or whose students have achieved exceptional success in co-curricular activities is never celebrated. School billboards often showcase the names and photos of students who score above 90 percent, creating a skewed image of student performance. By highlighting only the highest achievers, schools present a selective narrative that promotes academic excellence while ignoring the broader spectrum of student efforts and progress. This practice subtly pushes under the carpet those students who might not have achieved high marks but have shown improvement, creativity, or strength in non-academic areas. As a result, it fosters a culture of comparison and pressure, while marginalizing the achievements and needs of the majority who fall outside the narrow definition of "success."

In a student’s life, where a teacher’s attention and appreciation are the key factors for development, the silent crisis of the same is often overlooked. The student-teacher ratio is overwhelming and burdensome on teachers. With high numbers, assessing each student’s learning pace, offering individual feedback, or addressing emotional and psychological needs becomes an impossible task. Despite this, schools continue to project a false image of personalized education through marketing tactics. The reality on the ground is starkly different. Overcrowded classrooms, overburdened teachers, and one-size-fits-all teaching methodologies dominate the scene. This severely impacts student learning outcomes and their overall development.

Parents, in their pursuit of providing the "best" for their children, are also caught in this infrastructure-driven race, often without asking deeper questions. How experienced are the teachers? What are their teaching styles? What professional development do they receive? How does the school measure learning outcomes? These questions are frequently sidelined in favour of the more tangible and flashy criteria. This consumerist mindset pushes schools to invest more in cosmetic upgrades than academic quality.

It is high time that Indian schools and parents realigned their priorities. While infrastructure is undoubtedly important – especially for providing a safe and engaging environment – it cannot replace the fundamental role of a qualified and passionate teacher. Schools must invest in building robust teacher development programs, offer sustainable salaries, and create a culture where teachers are respected and empowered. Moreover, there is a pressing need for a cultural shift that places teachers at the centre of the educational discourse. Celebrating teacher achievements, involving them in curriculum decisions, and offering them platforms for sharing best practices can go a long way in improving the overall quality of education. As India aspires to become a global leader in education, it must look beyond the surface and invest in the people who matter most – the teachers.

Prachi Lahiri is a teacher with more than a decade's experience. She is a member of the Curricular Area Group on Social Science by the National Syllabus and Teaching-Learning Material Committee. Prachi’s research paper, titled “The Trials and Triumphs of the Indian Schoolteachers during the COVID-19 Pandemic,” was accepted by UNESCO under the Education for Resilience initiative, a platform of UNESCO’s International Institute for Educational Planning (IIEP). Another research paper by her titled “Impact and Influence of Teachers on the Lives of Migrant Children” was published in the Journal of Culture and Values in Education (JCVE), a peer-reviewed, open-access academic e-journal dedicated to cultural and educational research. She can be reached at lprachi@npshsr.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prachi Lahiri
Schools boast of their facilities, gyms, and pools, but what about the teachers? Prachi Lahiri calls out India’s obsession with infrastructure over classroom quality, arguing that trained teachers – not facades – drive meaningful learning and student growth.

item.title: Sexuality education in schools in India: a case study and lesson plan for teachers
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Ketaki Chowkhani

Gender and sexuality are some of the taboo topics within school education. They get relegated to gender sensitization programs and if possible, sex education programs, which are often conducted by external resource persons. In this article and the lesson plan that follows, I will explain some of the current debates around sexuality education that teachers and schools face vis a vis adolescents. This will draw from my book The Limits of Sexuality Education: Love, Sex, and Adolescent Masculinities in Urban India, where I discuss how conceptualizing adolescent masculinities can help us rethink sexuality education in urban India. The lesson plan will help teachers integrate ideas of gender and sexuality within the curriculum in a seamless manner, without resorting to embarrassment.

In my research, that included talking to teachers and analyzing school curriculum, I maintain that sexuality education, the way it is taught in schools – either through external resource persons, such as NGOs, or sex educators – is often aimed at middle school students. In my book, there are two chapters that would be of specific interest to school teachers. I would like to discuss these so that teachers are aware of the latest scholarship and debates within sexuality education and can make informed decisions within the classroom and in their teaching practice.

The first idea that will be of interest to teachers is an examination of the curriculum on sexuality education produced by different stakeholders. These include the State, feminist organizations, Christian organizations, and the sexologists’ community. Reviewing the curriculum produced by these different actors, I maintain that they form four discourses on sexuality education. My focus in reviewing the curriculum is to analyze how they understand questions of adolescent male desire, sexuality, pleasure, love, relationships, and romance. I arrive at the conclusion that:

The feminist discourse brings to the fore the social and political context of sexuality education, stressing a Rights-based discourse; the Christian organization brings in an extensive discussion of romance and love; the State discourse brings in the scientific and the moral discourse; and the sexological discourse, while located within the scientific, crucially brings in a discussion of sexual pleasure. I examine each of these discourses in detail, suggesting that individually each represents only a partial approach to sexuality education. (Chowkhani, 2024, 4).

The curriculum, I maintain, is not only for the middle school, but is also an adult imagination of what sexuality education must be. Teachers must be aware that we often impose our adult ideas on young people, especially teenagers, and that sometimes listening to them will help us teach them better. This is precisely what I explore in the other chapters, which examine adolescent male sexual knowledge and romance to show how our adult conceptualization of adolescent sexuality is limited, which makes our curriculum on sexuality also limited.

I also note that what is missing within the curriculum and discussion on sexuality education is a healthy discussion of love and romance. Sexual pleasure is discussed in various ways in the different discourses on sexuality education: through masturbation, orgasms, and female pleasure. But I maintain that there is a missing discourse of love within the curriculum. The Christian discourse teaches about love and romance, but their imagination is limited to heterosexual love and/or love within the same caste/class/religion. A wider, healthier imagination of love and romance is necessary to be included in the curriculum. In the lesson plan that follows, I discuss in detail how to include discussions of love within the school curriculum.

The other chapter that might be of interest to teachers in school is the one titled I am a teacher, I think the worst: teacher responsibilities and regulation of student romance in schools in Mumbai. This chapter contains interviews with teachers from different schools in Mumbai to understand how they perceive and negotiate with student romances. The aim is to understand the teacher’s position vis-a-vis students, the administration, and parents. Many of the teachers I interviewed were walking a tightrope, caught between students and school administrators who would hold them responsible should something happen to the students. The teachers were not averse to student romance, but they felt personally responsible should that spiral into a pregnancy or sexually transmitted diseases. As the title of the chapter suggests, they think the worst, making a direct leap from romance to pregnancy. In research on adolescent male romance, my interviews with young men suggested that they were very careful about having sexual intercourse with their girlfriends, and that some of them were genuinely happy just holding their girlfriends’ hands. This suggests that a lot of fears the teachers have about adolescent romance are rather unfounded. I maintain that schools and teachers are more concerned about the reputation of the school rather than the safety and health of the student. They seek to regulate students’ movements, clothes, and interactions based on an imagination of the depraved teenager, which I have already discussed does not hold true since adolescent boys are very thoughtful about sex, romance, and desire. Scandals in the school can lead to harsher punishments and restrictions on students. This leads to what Italian philosopher Giorgio Agamben calls a state of exception which is a permanent state of emergency in response to conflict. In the school setting, any scandal leads to a state of exception, where all students are governed by a sense of emergency at all times.

School teachers are especially alert to student romance during school trips, and have a tough time navigating this liminal space. The ones who are most comfortable with student romance and are able to deal with it with much grace and tact, are often school counsellors and psychologists. They play the role of the empathetic adult. My point here is that it is not easy being a teacher since one is caught between caring for students and caring for the reputation of the school.

My contention is that as teachers we need to rethink the gap between adults and adolescents. If we can demand of adolescents to be responsible like adults, we must also be open to the fact that we were once adolescents ourselves, and that maybe our adolescent desires have still not left us. This reduces the generational gap between adults and adolescents. One needs to not only question adolescents for their behaviour, but adults too; the latter is often left scot-free when it comes to apportioning blame for adolescent errors. This, I maintain, will help in also rethinking sexuality education, since we, as adults will not think of adolescents as the ‘other’ but understand them as part of ourselves, a self which was also young not so long ago.

Lesson plan

There are two ways to indirectly include sexuality education within the school curriculum. The first is in the literature class and the second in the art class.

Literature class, especially English classes

Prescribe to the students various texts from classic and contemporary literature in English, ranging from British, American, and Indian writing in English, which deal with love and romance. Examples could be romantic dramas from Shakespeare, novels from Jane Austen, or contemporary fiction from Shashi Deshpande. The teachers can also include more classical texts in translation such as Kalidasa’s poems Kumarasambhavam or Meghadutam, which have themes of love and longing. Teachers can also include readings from Ruth Vanita and Salim Kidwai’s book Same Sex Love in India, and Sufi poetry from Rumi, which explore themes of different forms of love. These texts, which include a literary repertoire of drama, novels, poetry, short stories, will expose students to a rich variety of love: love for a lover, love for the country, same sex love, love for god, love within the family, and so on. Teachers can also use bell hooks’ book All About Love as a guide to their teaching on love and romance, since love comes in all shapes, sizes, and forms. Teachers can also use audio-visual aids, especially cinema to discuss other forms of love, like love between siblings, or love between a parent and their child. Videos on consent can also be shown when discussing romance and love between two people.

Some of the class discussions and assignment can be around:

  1. What is love?
  2. How does one differentiate between different forms of love?
  3. What does one do when one is ‘in love’?
  4. What is consent?
  5. What do you feel when you are in love – how would you process these emotions?

Role play can be used in class to discuss different forms of love. Students can act out dramatic scenes from plays on love. Students should also be encouraged to understand what is acceptable in love – like care, affection, consent, respect, dignity, and what is not acceptable, like violence, force, coercion, jealousy. Role play can be used in these cases to understand love within a romantic relationship.

How is love connected to sexuality education? Relationships education, romance, and love, are an integral part of sexuality, and as I mentioned earlier, there is a missing discourse of love within the sexuality education curriculum. Love needn’t be addressed solely through a different sexuality education curriculum, but can be included within the larger curriculum of literary studies.

Art classes

The other way to discuss sexuality education is to talk about the erotics of the body and this can be done very well in the art class.

Explain to the students the proportions of the male and female body. This will expose students to the external genitalia without any embarrassment to the teacher or the student. Ask the students to study the human body in the nude, in different poses. This will help the student understand the shape and form of the human body. The following assignment can be given

  1. Draw the human body in the nude, in the following positions: standing, sitting, lying down.
  2. Draw the female and male body in the nude in the standing position, indicating the various parts of the body, including the external genitalia.

While biology classes can cover the internal genitalia and the physiology of pregnancy, contraception, puberty, and so on, art classes can cover the erotics of sexuality, which scholars of sexuality education around the world point out is missing within the curriculum.

Another way art classes can be used to study the erotics of sexuality is to use clay modeling to sculpt the human body. This can be done after the drawing classes, so that the students have already learnt about the proportions of the human body and face and are able to reproduce it in 3D. Ask the students to make a bust of a human being, understanding the dynamics of the human face. Next, ask the students to sculpt the human body. The following assignments can be given.

  1. Sculpt the human body in the nude – you can choose the male or female form.
  2. Sculpt the human face.

Why is it important to learn to sculpt the human body in the nude? This is so that the students can learn about the body through touch, not just through words and written text. Through touch, they can understand the curves and shapes of different human bodies, how the torso is differently shaped from the arms and legs, where the muscles are positioned, how the rib cage is important, where the external genitalia lie, and how the skeletal and bone structure of the human body is. This is to address the erotics of sexuality education in a classroom without embarrassment to the teacher or student, since it is done for the sake of art and to understand the human body. A clinical approach can also be taken to this exercise to make it less uncomfortable to everyone involved.

As mentioned earlier, biology classes can also take a clinical approach to sexuality education by talking about the physiology of the reproductive systems. This way, sexuality education is distributed within various subjects without needing to be separately taught as a subject. This helps in integrating sexuality education within the larger school curriculum, without having the embarrassment of teaching it as a separate subject. This is to aid both the teachers and students to tackle the subject of sexuality, sex, love, and romance in a ‘normal’ fashion rather than make it a matter of pride or shame.

Reference

Chowkhani, Ketaki. 2024. The Limits of Sexuality Education: Love, Sex, and Adolescent Masculinities in Urban India. New Delhi, Routledge.

Ketaki Chowkhani is Assistant Professor of Sociology at Manipal Institute of Social Sciences Humanities and Arts, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal, India. She teaches courses on Gender Studies and Singles Studies. She can be reached at kchowkhani@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ketaki Chowkhani
How can schools talk about sex education without awkwardness? Ketaki Chowkhani writes about how teachers can include respectful and meaningful discussions on gender, sexuality, and relationships in the classroom.

item.title: How to make classroom conversations collaborative
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Shabira Hussain
