I once witnessed a literature teacher facing some heat from her students. They were protesting because their answers were marked wrong: “But why? Our explanation is different, but it still makes sense.” A few more voices chimed in, not to explore the question, but to insist that their version had to be accepted. The teacher patiently replied, “You know 2+2=4, and that’s correct. But 3+1 is also 4, and so is 5-1. There are multiple ways to arrive at the same result, but sometimes one way is clearer and better than the others.”

Neither the students nor the teacher is wrong. Why then was there a disagreement?

This hit me during a recent circle time activity. Students were asked to brainstorm and present ways to survive on a deserted island without any external help. After the first group presented its strategies, the other groups started questioning and criticizing the strategies instead of exploring them further. Predictably, the group that was presenting got defensive. Within minutes, the discussion spiralled into something far from its intent – a full-blown DEBATE!

Sounds familiar?

We witness such scenes in our classrooms all the time. Students often feel like they must prove themselves right and others wrong. And let’s be honest – we teachers sometimes fall into this trap too.

But what if we flipped this? What if everyone is on the same side? What if we made space for conversations where multiple perspectives coexist without the need for a clear winner?

The island discussion could have been better managed by encouraging students to approach each other's ideas with curiosity rather than criticism, perhaps starting with questions like “What inspired this idea?” or “How could this work in different situations?”

As a debate coach, I spend plenty of time teaching students to build strong arguments and defend their viewpoints. But not every conversation needs to be a battle of wits. Sometimes, the real win is in understanding, not in winning.

Here are some practical strategies for teachers to transform debates into dialogues:

  1. Argument versus discussion: We often crib that students never follow instructions – though they were instructed to discuss, they ended up debating! But, what if they don’t know the difference? So, once in a while, have a conversation about it, or, better still, model both scenarios. With the help of other teachers or students, do a role play to show both scenarios and let the students realize the difference. This hands-on experience may lead to clarity.
  2. Set clear norms: Before beginning an activity or discussion, state – even if you have done this in the previous class – that students need to respectfully listen first, then reflect, restate, and ask questions to seek clarity. The responder should also pause, restate the question, and respond only to what is asked without being defensive.
  3. Promote curiosity over criticism: Students may sometimes dismiss others' answers as impossible or incorrect without considering the possibilities behind those ideas. When a student says, “That’s impossible” or “That’s not correct,” gently redirect the conversation by encouraging them to ask exploratory questions like, “What inspired your thinking?” or “How could we improve this idea?” This shift from criticism to curiosity fosters collaborative thinking and ensures a supportive environment where every student feels comfortable sharing their thoughts, knowing their ideas will be valued and refined rather than rejected.
  4. Facilitate time to pause and reflect: Giving students time to think and process ideas can increase their chances of considering or accepting different perspectives. Be open to the possibility that this reflection time may also lead to well-formed and stronger arguments. Encouraging thoughtful pauses supports deeper conversations and constructive dialogue. So, make a habit of giving students time to think whenever you hold discussions.
  5. Encourage active listening: Effective conversations begin with active listening – a skill often lacking in students today. To foster this, create opportunities for them to become intentional listeners rather than impulsive speakers. A simple yet impactful practice is to have every student restate or summarize a peer’s point before responding. This not only enhances comprehension but also cultivates empathy and respect for diverse perspectives.

By intentionally creating opportunities for dialogue, teachers can transform classroom discussions into meaningful learning experiences that build understanding, empathy, and collaborative thinking.

Shabira Hussain describes herself as a curious learner rather than a teacher. As the first girl from her village, Sikka (Jamnagar), to step out to study and work, she sees the world as her university, people as her professors, and each day as a new chapter. An amateur writer, she is the author of My Green Self, inspired by her love for planters and plants. She regularly shares her reflections on LinkedIn. She can be reached at shabirahussain123@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Shabira Hussain
Classroom discussions don’t have to be debates. Shabira Banu shows how active listening, reflection, and clear norms can transform arguments into collaborative conversations that value every student’s perspective.

item.dc:creator: shalini

item.title: How do you write?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Deepak Manjraker

Writing is an art, yet many students see it merely as a tool for scoring marks. Literature is often taught with limited objectives, focusing on exam performance rather than skill development. Teaching methods overlook the broader applications of literature, such as contextual understanding, critical thinking, and communication skills. The “general-to-specific” writing technique offers a constructive solution, fostering clarity, coherence, and engagement. This article explores its significance, structure, and advantages while aligning with the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, which emphasizes structured writing for critical thinking and academic success.

Literature review

Scholars emphasize the role of structured writing in improving comprehension. Smith (2020) states that the general-to-specific method enhances readability and prevents ambiguity. Jones (2019) highlights its effectiveness in expository writing, ensuring logical progression. Brown (2021) finds that students using this method perform better in assessments. NEP 2020 (Paragraph 4-13, Section 4, School Education) supports structured writing techniques for developing communication and critical thinking skills.

Research and scope

So how well are schools training their students to write? A research was undertaken as a qualitative classroom-based study aimed at exploring the pedagogical effectiveness of the general-to-specific writing technique in English Literature classrooms at the secondary and senior secondary levels. It involved analyzing student-written responses, essays, and examination scripts from CBSE English courses to assess the coherence, logical flow, and depth of analysis achieved through structured writing. The study also reviewed how this approach aligns with NEP 2020’s focus on enhancing language, analytical, and writing competencies among learners.

Results

Our findings indicate that the general-to-specific writing technique significantly enhances students’ ability to express ideas clearly, logically, and coherently. Writing from a general perspective before narrowing down to specific details helps in establishing context, improving retention, and strengthening arguments. It enables learners to connect broad concepts with concrete examples, thus making their writing more persuasive and academically sound.

The study also reveals that this technique benefits students not only at the school level but throughout their academic and professional journey. While in school, it supports the development of foundational skills such as organization, clarity, and critical thinking; beyond school, it equips students with the analytical and linguistic competence necessary for higher education and professional writing.

Key benefits

  1. Structured and logical flow

Beginning with a general statement and gradually progressing toward specific details ensures clarity, logical coherence, and reader engagement across disciplines and age groups.

  1. Improved argumentation and critical thinking

Students learn to develop well-organized arguments, move from theory to evidence, and think critically about connections between ideas – skills that remain valuable in university and research writing.

  1. Clarity in explanation

Complex concepts are made more accessible when writers introduce general overviews before discussing detailed examples, a technique equally useful in literature, science, law, or social studies.

  1. Effective preparation for higher education

The general-to-specific approach builds the foundation for academic essay writing, thesis development, and research-based argumentation, thereby easing the transition from school to university studies.

  1. Alignment with educational policy

NEP 2020 emphasizes the need to train students in structured and coherent writing methods to enhance their analytical and critical reasoning abilities – a goal directly supported by this technique.

In essence, the general-to-specific writing technique nurtures lifelong writing and reasoning skills. Students who learn to apply it in school are better prepared to write effectively in higher education, competitive examinations, and professional fields that demand structured thinking and clear expression.

Discussion

These findings reinforce the importance of structured writing. In scientific writing, general theories precede specific experiments. Business reports present market overviews before company-specific data. Essay writing benefits from thesis statements followed by supporting arguments. NEP 2020 emphasizes structured writing to develop clear articulation and logical reasoning.

Examples from CBSE literature texts

  1. From Lost Spring by Anees Jung
    1. Q: What social issues does the story highlight?
    1. Ans: (General to specific)

Kailash Satyarthi, social activist and Nobel laureate says, “Give a child love, laughter, and peace, not child labour.” India being a country with the largest youth population in the world must preserve, train, and use this important human resource. Child labour is a curse not only for the poor, but for the entire society.

The story Lost Spring highlights the issues of poverty and child labour in India. It describes how children like Saheb and Mukesh are deprived of basic education and forced into exploitative labour due to financial hardships. Saheb, a rag picker in Seemapuri, dreams of a better life but is trapped in a cycle of poverty, while Mukesh, a child labourer in the bangle-making industry, aspires to break free from this generational occupation.

  • From Two Gentlemen of Verona by A.J. Cronin
    • Q: What qualities of the two boys, Nicola and Jacopo, impress the narrator?
    • Ans: (General to specific)

Hard work, responsibility, and selflessness are qualities that define truly noble individuals. Eric Berne, a Canadian psychiatrist, in his famous theory of the Three Ego States says that a person displays specific traits not because of their age but because of the way they perceive their life. Similarly, a person displays maturity not because they have attained a certain age, but because of how they handle their life.

Nicola and Jacopo, despite their young age, display remarkable dedication and perseverance. They take on various odd jobs – selling newspapers, polishing shoes, and running errands – not for personal gain but to support their ailing sister, Lucia. Their quiet determination and unwavering sense of duty deeply impress the narrator.

Conclusion

The general-to-specific technique enhances clarity, coherence, and engagement in academic writing. It fosters logical reasoning and analytical skills, as emphasized by NEP 2020. Future research could explore its effectiveness across learning styles and writing formats. In addition to structured writing, appropriate vocabulary and sentence construction are crucial for impactful answers.

References

  • Brown, A. (2021). Effective Academic Writing Techniques. Oxford University Press.
  • Jones, R. (2019). The Power of Logical Progression in Writing. Cambridge Press.
  • Smith, L. (2020). Clarity in Communication: Strategies for Writers. Harvard Press.
  • Government of India. (2020). National Education Policy 2020. Ministry of Education.

Deepak Manjrekar is a PGT at Anandalaya Education Society in Anand, Gujarat and has been teaching English for the past 22 years. Throughout his career, his focus has been to make English literature and language education easy for his students by trying to find immersive and inclusive teaching methods and taking literature beyond textbooks and classrooms. His approach to English Literature is both passionate and practical as he helps his students feel the pleasure of the text while also helping them score better in exams. He can be reached at deepak0421@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Deepak Manjraker
Writing answers doesn’t have to be chaotic. Deepak Manjraker shows how the general-to-specific method works across subjects, and why NEP 2020 highlights it as a key skill for analytical and linguistic development.
